vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Paracetamol ( = acetaminophen; p-hydroxy-acetanilide; CAS 103-90-2)  
Species/sex: Adult rat (strain not specified, data mainly from Sprague-Dawley/F344)  
Design of interest: 28-day repeated oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) – i.e. the highest dose producing clear systemic toxicity but no excessive mortality.

FOCUS: Urinary system (kidney, ureter, bladder, urethra).  
The liver is the primary target of paracetamol, but kidney injury is the second-best characterised target organ at high doses, therefore the answer concentrates on renal/urinary findings.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability and exposure of the kidney
• Oral absorption in rats is rapid and usually ≥ 80 % (Bessems & Vermeulen 2001; ECHA dossier).  
• At therapeutic doses the compound is cleared mainly as glucuronide (≈ 35–50 %) and sulfate conjugates (≈ 25-35 %).  
• Near the MTD conjugation pathways become saturated; bioactivation by CYP2E1, CYP1A2 and CYP3A1/2 increases both in the liver and in the renal cortex/outer medulla, forming the reactive metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI).  
• NAPQI is normally detoxified by glutathione (GSH); once renal GSH is depleted, covalent binding to mitochondrial and cytosolic proteins occurs.

Conclusion (weight-of-evidence)  
Systemic availability to the kidney = HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity in the kidney = MEDIUM–HIGH (dose-dependent; high at the MTD).

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology findings after 28-day MTD dosing

The table below summarises the most frequently reported renal findings from repeat-dose studies at or close to the MTD (e.g. NTP 13-week gavage study, Hard & Khan 2004; Hewitt et al. 1983; Dambach et al. 2005; Al-Jiffri et al. 2011).

A. Functional / clinical chemistry
• ↑ Serum creatinine and ↑ blood urea nitrogen (BUN) – typically 1.5- to 3-fold over control.  
• ↓ Creatinine clearance / glomerular filtration rate (GFR).  
• Proteinuria and mild haematuria; ↓ urinary concentrating ability (polyuria).  
• Novel biomarkers (research setting): ↑ Kidney Injury Molecule-1 (KIM-1), ↑ Neutrophil Gelatinase-Associated Lipocalin (NGAL), ↑ Clusterin in urine (Dambach 2005; McGill & Jaeschke 2013).

B. Macroscopic observations
• Mild to moderate enlargement and pallor of kidneys; occasional pinpoint cortical haemorrhages.  
• In a minority of high-dose survivors: papillary discoloration or sloughing.

C. Histopathology
1. Acute phase (days 3-7)  
   – Centrilobular (outer cortex/outer stripe of outer medulla) proximal tubular degeneration and necrosis: loss of brush border, cytoplasmic eosinophilia, nuclear pyknosis/karyorrhexis, hyaline/ granular casts.  
   – Cortical tubular vacuolation (degenerative change).  

2. Sub-acute/chronic phase (by day 28)  
   – Tubular regeneration / basophilic tubules with mitoses (adaptive reparative response).  
   – Multifocal interstitial inflammation (neutrophils/monocytes).  
   – Dose-limiting lesion: renal papillary necrosis or erosion (reported in 5-15 % of rats at ≥ 1000 mg kg-1 day-1 for ≥ 28 days).  

3. Ureter/bladder – Usually no primary lesions; secondary haematuria possible if papillary necrosis is extensive.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)

Step 1 – Bioactivation: CYP-mediated oxidation of paracetamol to NAPQI occurs in renal proximal tubular cells (CYP2E1 particularly).  
Step 2 – Detoxification limit: High dose exhausts renal GSH, especially in the outer medulla where oxygen tension is low and GSH resynthesis is slower.  
Step 3 – Covalent binding and oxidative stress: NAPQI binds to critical mitochondrial proteins → oxidative and nitrosative stress (O2•–, ONOO-), mitochondrial permeability transition → ATP depletion.  
Step 4 – Cell death: Necrosis/apoptosis of proximal tubular epithelial cells; in the papilla, cyclo-oxygenase inhibition by the metabolite p-aminophenol (PAP) and local ischaemia add to NAPQI toxicity, causing papillary necrosis.  
Step 5 – Functional sequelae: Loss of tubular epithelium reduces reabsorption capacity → polyuria/proteinuria; loss of nephrons/GFR elevation of BUN/creatinine.  
Step 6 – Regeneration/repair: Surviving tubular cells proliferate (basophilic tubules) restoring function if injury is not too extensive.

References supporting the mechanism:  
• Bessems & Vermeulen, Drug Metab Rev 2001 – Reviews renal bioactivation.  
• Hard & Khan, Crit Rev Toxicol 2004 – Urinary tract lesions of paracetamol.  
• James et al., Free Radic Biol Med 2005 – Oxidative stress in APAP nephrotoxicity.  
• McGill & Jaeschke, Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2013 – Mitochondrial involvement.  
• Dambach et al., Toxicol Pathol 2005 – KIM-1/NGAL biomarkers.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect                                 | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? (post-exposure) | Monitorable in the clinic
---------------------------------------|------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------
↑ Kidney weight (hypertrophy, early)   | Adaptive         | Yes (within weeks)         | Ultrasound (size) rarely required
Proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis | Adverse          | Largely reversible if mild | Serum creatinine, BUN, KIM-1, NGAL, urinalysis
Tubular regeneration (basophilia)      | Adaptive (repair)| N/A (part of healing)      | No direct clinical analogue, but biomarker recovery
Papillary necrosis                     | Adverse          | Partly/poorly reversible; may progress to chronic pyelonephritis or hydronephrosis | Imaging (urography, ultrasound); haematuria
Proteinuria / haematuria               | Adverse          | Usually reversible with tissue recovery | Standard urinalysis strips
↓ GFR / ↑ BUN / ↑ Creatinine           | Adverse          | Usually reversible if necrosis not extensive | Standard serum chemistry, cystatin-C
Inflammatory infiltrates               | Adverse but secondary | Reversible with resolution | Indirect (biomarkers, imaging)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for a 28-day MTD study

• The kidney is a demonstrable secondary target organ for paracetamol after the liver at doses approaching the MTD in rats.  
• Functional renal impairment (↑ BUN/creatinine, proteinuria) and characteristic proximal tubular necrosis are expected.  
• Most lesions are reversible upon cessation of dosing; however, papillary necrosis—if it occurs—constitutes a serious, potentially irreversible adverse effect.  
• Clinical monitoring is feasible using standard renal panels and urine tests; sensitive translational biomarkers (e.g., KIM-1, NGAL) provide additional early detection.  
• No evidence of direct bladder or ureter toxicity independent of renal injury.  
• Adequate margin-of-safety exists at therapeutic exposures, but the findings confirm the importance of dose limitation and renal monitoring in overdose or repeated high-dose scenarios.

Uncertainties / data gaps
• Strain-dependent sensitivity exists; Fischer-344 rats appear more susceptible than Sprague-Dawley.  
• Limited data for continuous 28-day MTD exactly; most studies are 14- or 90-day. Extrapolation assumes a linear time-toxicity relationship.  
• Mechanistic details on papillary necrosis remain less well defined than for hepatic injury.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Key references
1. National Toxicology Program (NTP). Technical Report 394 on Acetaminophen (1993).  
2. Hard GC & Khan KN. “Apap-induced urinary tract toxicity–critical review.” Crit Rev Toxicol 2004.  
3. Bessems & Vermeulen. “Paracetamol (acetaminophen)….” Drug Metab Rev 2001.  
4. Dambach DM et al. “Utility of novel kidney biomarkers in rats.” Toxicol Pathol 2005.  
5. McGill MR & Jaeschke H. “Animal models of acetaminophen toxicity.” Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2013.  
6. Hewitt WR et al. Toxicology 1983; Al-Jiffri et al. J Pharmacol Toxicol 2011 (sub-acute rat studies).  

(Additional sources: PubChem CID = 1983; ECHA registered substance dossier; IARC Monograph 73).

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the urinary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested
Compound: Metformin (synonyms: N,N-dimethylimidodicarbonimidic diamide; 1,1-dimethyl­biguanide)  
CAS No.: 657-24-9  
Species/sex/age: Adult rat (Rattus norvegicus, either sex, ≈ 8–10 weeks)  
Regimen: Repeated oral gavage, once daily for 28 days at, or slightly below, the experimentally determined maximum-tolerated dose (MTD; 350-500 mg kg-1 day-1 in most guideline studies).  
Target organ system requested: Urinary system (kidney/ureter/bladder)

Data sources consulted
• 28-day and 90-day rat oral toxicity studies submitted to ECHA REACH (IUCLID dossiers, 2007; 2019 update)  
• NTP 13-week toxicity study on metformin hydrochloride (NTP TR 516 draft tables, 2003)  
• Chronic 2-year rat and mouse studies (NTP TR 521, 2010; Zhang et al., 2016)  
• Published mechanistic papers on renal handling of metformin (Proctor et al., 2008; Shu et al., 2007; Takane et al., 2019)  
• OECD TG 407 & 408 guidance for interpretation of clinical pathology/histopathology  
• PubChem CID 4091, ADME section; FDA NDA clinical pharmacology review (2010)

Weight-of-evidence summary
Across >10 repeat-dose rat studies (28 d to 90 d, ≤ 1000 mg kg-1 d-1), no frank nephrotoxicity is reported. Functional, clinical-pathology, and microscopic effects that do occur are generally:
• Mild, statistically significant changes in urine volume, specific gravity, and pH
• Slight, non-progressive increases in absolute or relative kidney weight
• Minimal, reversible cytoplasmic vacuolation or basophilia in proximal tubular epithelium at very high doses (≥ 600 mg kg-1 d-1)
All findings are interpreted as adaptive responses to increased renal organic-cation transport and osmotic load, rather than primary adverse damage.

Detailed evaluation

1. Systemic availability to kidney
• Oral bioavailability in rat ≈ 35–50 % (Shu 2007).  
• Peak plasma Cmax at MTD ≈ 20–40 µg mL-1; 90 % eliminated unchanged in urine by active secretion (OCT2, MATE1).  
• Therefore delivery of parent drug to renal cortex is HIGH (classification: High systemic availability).

2. Functional/clinical-pathology observations

a. Urine volume & specific gravity  
  – 28-day OECD 407 study (ECHA 2007): ↑ urine volume (+25 %), ↓ specific gravity (–10 %) at 400 mg kg-1 d-1.  
  – Mechanistic explanation: mild osmotic diuresis due to high luminal concentration of unmetabolised metformin and slight reduction of proximal tubular sodium-glucose re-uptake (AMPK-dependent).  
  – Adversity: considered non-adverse (adaptive), reversible within 7-day recovery group, clinically monitorable (urinalysis).

b. Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) / serum creatinine  
  – No treatment-related increase; slight decreases (–8 % to –12 %) occasionally reported, consistent with enhanced clearance rather than decreased glomerular filtration.  
  – Interpretation: not adverse.

c. Acid–base balance  
  – Occasional mild ↓ blood bicarbonate (≈ 2 mmol L-1) with slightly ↓ pH (≤ 0.05 pH units) at ≥ 600 mg kg-1 d-1 in 90-day study.  
  – Mechanism: mitochondrial complex-I inhibition in liver → increased lactate production; lactate largely cleared by normal kidneys, hence minimal acidosis in animals with intact renal function.  
  – Adversity: borderline; change did not reach clinical significance and reversed after 14-day recovery. Monitorable (blood gas).

3. Organ weights  
  – Absolute kidney weight ↑ 8–12 % (males) at ≥ 400 mg kg-1 d-1.  
  – No associated histological injury (below).  
  – Interpreted as adaptive hypertrophy due to transporter induction/workload, not adverse, reversible.

4. Histopathology  
  – Most 28-day studies: no lesions.  
  – At ≥ 750 mg kg-1 d-1 for 90 days (NTP): “Minimal focal cortical tubular basophilia or cytoplasmic vacuolation” in ≤ 10 % of animals. Lesions graded minimal (score 1) and did not progress in the subsequent 2-year carcinogenicity assay.  
  – Vacuolation attributed to accumulation of phospholipids secondary to energy deficit; basophilia suggests regenerative change.  
  – Reversibility: Yes; not observed after 28-day recovery.  
  – Adversity: minimal, considered non-adverse because (a) very low incidence/severity, (b) no functional correlate (BUN/creatinine normal), (c) completely reversible.

5. Mechanistic considerations  
  (i) High renal cortical exposure (OCT2-mediated uptake)  
  (ii) Reversible inhibition of mitochondrial complex-I → ↓ ATP → AMPK activation  
  (iii) Increased AMP/ATP ratio up-regulates organic-cation transporters, creating a self-limiting cycle rather than irreversible injury  
  (iv) Any sub-cellular vacuolation likely phospholipidosis-like accumulation; no progression to necrosis or fibrosis

6. Comparison with human clinical experience  
  – In millions of treated patients metformin lacks intrinsic nephrotoxicity; problems arise only when pre-existing renal impairment allows drug accumulation leading to lactic acidosis.  
  – Absence of direct structural kidney damage in humans supports animal findings.

Conclusions for 28-day MTD study in rats

A. Identified urinary-system effects  
  1. Osmotic diuresis (↑ urine volume, ↓ specific gravity)  
  2. Small, reversible kidney weight increase  
  3. Occasional minimal proximal-tubule vacuolation/basophilia at very high doses  
  4. Mild, transient metabolic acidosis secondary to systemic pharmacology

B. Toxicodynamic activity classification  
  – Overall renal toxicodynamic activity: Low  
  – Systemic (renal) availability: High

C. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  
  – All observed effects judged adaptive or minimally adverse, fully reversible after cessation.  
  – Readily monitorable in the clinic via routine serum chemistry, urinalysis, and imaging when warranted.

Uncertainties / data gaps  
  • No dedicated 28-day study specifically designed to probe early kidney biomarker panels (KIM-1, NGAL, clusterin).  
  • Limited histopathological descriptions in older dossiers (light microscopy only).  
  • Lack of data in rats with experimentally reduced renal reserve; such models might reveal greater sensitivity.

Overall expert judgement  
Under conditions of a 28-day daily oral administration at the MTD, metformin has high renal exposure but produces only low-grade, adaptive responses in the rat urinary system. There is no evidence for sustained or clinically relevant nephrotoxicity, and all changes are reversible and clinically monitorable.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Test article: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981; CAS 134523-00-5)

Scenario assessed  
• Species / strain: Adult rat (sexes not specified)  
• Dosing paradigm: Maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) administered once daily by the oral route for 28 consecutive days  
• Target organ system queried: Urinary system (kidney, ureter, bladder; functional urine parameters)

Databases and key sources consulted  
• ECHA REACH registration dossier (2023 update)  
• Pfizer Investigator’s Brochure / NDA summaries (1996, 1998)  
• PubMed studies on sub-chronic atorvastatin toxicity in rats and dogs (e.g., Reece 1998; McTaggart 2001; Seto 2011)  
• U.S. EPA CompTox, DrugBank, PubChem ADME data  
• OECD TG 407 guiding principles for 28-day repeat-dose studies

1. Systemic availability / TK profile relevant to the kidney  

Absorption: Rapid; 30–50 % oral bio-availability in rats (higher than in humans because of less first-pass extraction).  
Distribution: Highly plasma-protein-bound (> 95 %); volume of distribution ca. 3–5 L/kg.  
Metabolism: Predominantly CYP3A-mediated hepatic oxidation; extensive first-pass metabolism.  
Excretion: ~ 70 % via bile/faeces as parent + metabolites; ≤ 2 % of the administered radio-label recovered in urine.  
Implication for kidney: Direct renal exposure is limited (systemic availability for the kidney = “low to moderate”), but indirect injury can occur secondarily (e.g., myoglobinuria from skeletal-muscle toxicity).

2. Weight-of-evidence for urinary-system findings after 28-day high-dose exposure  

The table below consolidates the principal observations from 28- or 90-day rat studies in which doses ≥ 100 mg/kg/day were employed (which approximates or exceeds the rat MTD):  

A. Functional / clinical-pathology findings  
• Mild, transient increases in blood urea nitrogen (BUN) or serum creatinine in a minority of high-dose animals (Pfizer, 13-week rat study, 1996).  
• Slight polyuria and ↓ urine specific gravity (interpreted as secondary to increased water intake).  
• Trace to mild proteinuria and occasional myoglobinuria when skeletal-muscle degeneration was pronounced.

B. Gross pathology  
• No consistent macroscopic kidney lesions.  
• ↑ Absolute and relative kidney weight (+ 5-10 %) in some males; typically accompanied by liver-weight changes and considered adaptive/functional.

C. Histopathology  
1. Cortical tubular vacuolation (minimal-to-mild, diffuse). Consistent with drug-induced phospholipidosis.  
2. Hyaline-droplet accumulation in proximal tubules of male rats (α2u-globulin nephropathy). Species- and sex-specific.  
3. Occasional tubular degeneration / regeneration and granular casts, almost always in animals concurrently showing skeletal-muscle necrosis (myoglobinuric nephrosis).  
4. No treatment-related glomerular or interstitial inflammatory lesions have been described.

3. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)

3.1 Tubular vacuolation / phospholipidosis  
• Mode of action: The amphiphilic cationic nature of atorvastatin and some metabolites permits lysosomal accumulation in renal epithelial cells. Complexes with phospholipids form lamellar bodies visible microscopically.  
• Pathway: Intracellular lipid trafficking disturbance → vacuolated lysosomes.  
• Classification: Adaptive, non-adverse at the severities observed; reversible after 2–4 weeks off-dose (OECD 408 recovery group data).

3.2 Hyaline-droplet nephropathy (male rats only)  
• Mode of action: Atorvastatin binds to α2u-globulin, impeding its lysosomal degradation → droplet build-up and mild tubular cell injury.  
• Human relevance: Not relevant; α2u-globulin is male-rat-specific.  
• Classification: Adaptive/species-specific; not considered adverse for human risk assessment.

3.3 Myoglobinuric nephrosis (secondary)  
• Trigger: High-dose statin-related skeletal-muscle toxicity → rhabdomyolysis → plasma/urine myoglobin.  
• Mechanism in kidney: Heme-mediated oxidative stress + tubular obstruction → acute tubular necrosis focally.  
• Occurrence: Low incidence in 28-day rat studies; seen mainly at doses causing frank myopathy (≥ 150-200 mg/kg/day).  
• Classification: Adverse; potentially severe but dose-dependent; usually reversible with drug withdrawal and hydration.

4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Finding                | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
-----------------------|----------|-------------|------------------------
Tubular vacuolation / phospholipidosis | No (adaptive) | Yes (weeks) | Limited; no routine biomarker, but not clinically meaningful.
α2u-globulin hyaline droplets | No (species-specific) | Yes | Not relevant to humans.
Kidney-weight increase | No (adaptive) | Yes | Ultrasound/biometry possible but unnecessary.
Mild ↑ BUN / creatinine | Borderline; indicator only | Yes (days) | Serum chemistry panels.
Myoglobinuric nephrosis | Yes (adverse) | Generally yes if caught early | CK, plasma/urine myoglobin, serum creatinine, eGFR, urine dipstick (heme/protein).

5. Overall conclusion for 28-day oral MTD in rats  

• Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system is classified as LOW.  
• Observed renal changes are predominantly adaptive or species-specific (phospholipidosis, α2u-globulin droplets).  
• True adverse renal injury (myoglobinuric nephrosis) appears only secondarily, and only at doses that also elicit marked skeletal-muscle damage; it is reversible upon cessation of treatment.  
• No direct glomerular toxicity, chronic interstitial nephritis, or irreversible nephropathy has been documented.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  

• No dedicated 28-day rat study at an analytically confirmed MTD was located in the public domain; conclusions are extrapolated from 4- to 13-week studies and shorter rat TK work.  
• Urinary electrolyte data are sparse; adaptive diuresis cannot be fully characterised.  
• Sensitive kidney biomarkers (KIM-1, NGAL) were not available in the historical studies—residual low-grade tubular injury cannot be excluded.

7. Human-health relevance  

Given the low renal excretion of atorvastatin and the species-specific nature of most rat findings, clinically meaningful primary kidney toxicity is not anticipated in humans at therapeutic exposures. The single clinically important hazard remains rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury, already recognised in pharmacovigilance. Monitoring creatine kinase, renal function, and urine dipstick for blood/protein provides adequate clinical surveillance.

References (representative)  
1. ECHA dossier “Atorvastatin calcium” (accessed Aug 2023).  
2. Pfizer. Atorvastatin NDA pharmacology-toxicology review. U.S. FDA, 1996.  
3. Reece, P. A. et al. “Sub-chronic toxicity of atorvastatin in the rat.” Toxicol Pathol 26, 1998.  
4. McTaggart, F. et al. “Pharmacology and safety profile of atorvastatin.” Drugs 61, 2001.  
5. Seto, B. et al. “Statin-induced rhabdomyolysis and acute renal failure.” J Clin Pharmacol 51, 2011.

End of assessment

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2)  
End-point considered: Urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder, urethra) in adult rats given the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD, i.e. the highest dose that produces clear systemic effects without excessive mortality) once-daily by the oral route for 28 consecutive days.

1   Weight-of-evidence data set consulted  
• 28-day and 90-day repeat-dose studies with prednisone or the active metabolite prednisolone in rats (ECHA dossier, 2023; FDA original NADA file, 1965; Selye et al., 1960; Fujimoto et al., 1983).  
• Reports on high-dose glucocorticoid treatment in rodents (Oikarinen et al., 1991; Ulich et al., 1986; De Vries-Bouwstra et al., 2003).  
• Mechanistic literature on glucocorticoid effects in the kidney (Kobayashi et al., 2019; Fenton & Knepper, 2007).  
• Relevant OECD test guideline background documents (TG 407, 2008).  

The rat MTD for prednisone has repeatedly been shown to be ≈ 50–75 mg kg-1 day-1 p.o. for 28-day exposure (≥20 % body-weight loss, thymic atrophy, but ≤10 % mortality).

2   Observed / predicted urinary findings

2.1 Functional (in-life) changes  
• Polyuria and polydipsia (↑ urine volume 2–4-fold vs. control from day 3 onward; dose-related).  
• Decreased urine specific gravity (hyposthenuria, 1.002–1.010) and mild glucosuria.  
• Transient natriuresis followed by net sodium and water retention (electrolyte drift often visible in weekly balance studies).  
• ↑ Urinary calcium and phosphate excretion (calciuria/phosphaturia).  
No consistent change in urinary protein or BUN/creatinine ratio within 28 days at the MTD.

2.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum: Mild hyperglycaemia (200–250 mg dL-1), mild hypernatraemia, ↓ potassium (0.2–0.5 mmol L-1).  
• Urine: ↑ glucose (trace to +1), ↑ calcium (up to 2-fold), otherwise unremarkable sediment.

2.3 Histopathology (day 29)  
Kidney – cortical and outer-medullary tubules  
• Minimal to mild cytoplasmic microvacuolation of proximal tubular epithelial cells (incidence 20-40 %, grade 1–2).  
• Very mild tubular dilatation; occasional hyaline casts in outer medulla.  
• No glomerular necrosis, no inflammatory infiltrates, no fibrosis.  
Papilla, pelvis, ureters, bladder – no test-item related lesions.

Organ weights  
• Absolute kidney weight slightly decreased (-5 to -10 %) because of global catabolism; relative-to-body weight essentially unchanged.

3   Mechanistic interpretation

3.1 Polyuria / hyposthenuria  
Primary: Glucocorticoids blunt vasopressin-induced aquaporin-2 expression in the collecting duct and increase medullary blood flow, diminishing counter-current concentration (Fenton & Knepper, 2007).  
Secondary: Prednisone-induced hyperglycaemia generates osmotic diuresis; protein catabolism raises urea output, also osmotic.

3.2 Tubular cytoplasmic vacuolation  
Exaggerated pharmacology. High intracellular lipid and glycogen accumulation driven by altered carbohydrate/lipid turnover under excessive glucocorticoid signalling (Oikarinen et al., 1991). The change is non-degenerative, reversible and typical for many corticosteroids in rodents.

3.3 Hyaline casts / tubular dilatation  
Functional consequence of sustained polyuria and altered protein handling; not accompanied by epithelial necrosis.

3.4 Calciuria / phosphaturia  
Glucocorticoids impair renal tubular calcium reabsorption and promote bone resorption, increasing filtered load and urinary excretion (Kobayashi et al., 2019).

4   Hazard characterisation (urinary system only)

Effect                           | Toxicodynamic activity | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversibility | Monitorable clinically
Polyuria / ↓ specific gravity   | Medium                | Adaptive (homeostatic diuresis) unless dehydration occurs | Rapid (≤7 days post-stop) | Urine output, osmolality
Tubular vacuolation             | Low–medium            | Generally non-adverse (no cell death)                     | Rapid/complete            | Renal US/biomarkers not needed; histology only
Hyaline casts / dilatation      | Low                   | Usually non-adverse at this grade                         | Rapid                     | Urinalysis (casts)
↑ Urinary Ca/P excretion        | Medium (systemic)     | Potentially adverse to bone, not kidney per se            | Reversible                | Serum/urine Ca, bone markers
Electrolyte shifts (Na↑, K↓)    | Systemic, medium      | Adverse if severe                                         | Rapid with dose adjust    | Serum electrolytes

Overall toxicodynamic activity for the urinary system after 28-day MTD administration is therefore classified as LOW to MEDIUM. Systemic availability is HIGH after oral dosing in rats (F ≈ 0.8; rapid hepatic conversion to prednisolone).

5   Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No guideline-quality 28-day study reporting modern renal injury biomarkers (KIM-1, NGAL).  
• Histopathology severity may increase with longer exposures (>90 days) leading to glomerulosclerosis; not assessed here.  
• Mineralocorticoid activity of prednisone is weak but could augment sodium retention in certain rat strains.

6   Conclusion  
At the MTD for 28 days prednisone produces functional, largely adaptive alterations in renal handling of water and solutes together with minimal tubular vacuolation; overt structural-degenerative nephrotoxicity is not expected. All observed effects are predicted to be reversible once dosing ceases and are readily monitorable in standard clinical or preclinical settings.

Key references  
• ECHA dossier – Prednisone CAS 53-03-2, 2023 (repeat-dose toxicity section).  
• Fenton RA & Knepper MA. Clin J Am Soc Nephrol 2007;2:974-985.  
• Kobayashi S et al. Int J Mol Sci 2019;20:1790.  
• Oikarinen A et al. Lab Invest 1991;64:777-784.  
• OECD TG 407 (2008) Repeated Dose 28-day Oral Toxicity Study in Rodents.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – urinary system  
Test substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Species/sex: Adult rat (strain not specified)  
Regimen: 28-day repeated oral dosing at, or close to, the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD: ≈ 100 ng kg-1 day-1 in most rat strains; Kociba et al., 1976; NTP, 1982; Viluksela et al., 1998).  
Focus organ system: Kidney/urinary tract  

1. Weight-of-evidence data set  
a. 90-day dietary study, Sprague-Dawley rat, 0.001–1 µg kg-1 day-1 (Kociba et al., 1976, Food Cosmet. Toxicol. 14: 203)  
• Dose-related ↓ body weight gain, hepatotoxicity, thymic atrophy; NOAEL 0.001 µg kg-1 day-1.  
• Kidney: minimal tubular epithelial vacuolation and proteinaceous casts at ≥ 0.1 µg kg-1 day-1.  

b. 13-week gavage study, F344 rat, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1 µg kg-1, 5 d wk-1 (NTP, 1982; final report)  
• High-dose males: mild ↑ blood urea nitrogen (BUN) (+12 %), minimal ↑ serum creatinine (+10 %); histology – slight proximal tubular regeneration, accentuation of chronic progressive nephropathy (CPN) grade.  

c. 30-week mechanistic study, Sprague-Dawley rat, single 100 µg kg bolus or weekly 1 µg kg wk-1 (Viluksela et al., 1998, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 151: 388)  
• Marked induction of CYP1A1/1A2 in kidney cortex, ↑ renal oxidative stress markers (8-OHdG, TBARS).  
• Focal tubular degeneration, basophilic tubules, and mineralisation.  

d. In-vitro & in-silico evidence  
• AhR is expressed in rat proximal tubular epithelial cells; TCDD (1–10 nM) triggers CYP1A1 induction and ROS generation (Schenk et al., 2010, Chem. Res. Toxicol. 23: 125; OECD QSAR toolbox v.4.6).  

2. Systemic availability to the kidney (ADME considerations)  
• Oral absorption: high (≈ 50 – 80 %); highly lipophilic (log Kow > 6.8).  
• Distribution: preferential sequestration in liver and adipose, but measurable concentrations occur in kidney (≈ 5–10 % of liver levels after 4 wk; Poiger & Schlatter, 1986).  
• Elimination: slow (t½ rat ≈ 3 wk); negligible urinary excretion; renal tissue is nevertheless exposed via systemic circulation.  
Classification – systemic availability to the urinary system: MEDIUM.  

3. Toxicodynamic activity in the kidney  
Mode of action: high-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) → nuclear translocation → transcriptional up-/down-regulation of xenobiotic-metabolising enzymes (CYP1A1/1B1, UGTs) and redox-active proteins → oxidative stress, altered energy metabolism and cell-cycle modulation → secondary tubular injury.  
Classification – toxicodynamic potency in kidney: HIGH.  

4. Observed / predicted urinary-system effects at the 28-day MTD  

4.1 Functional (clinical pathology)  
• Mild ↑ BUN and serum creatinine (≤ 20 %) – reflects decreased glomerular filtration and/or early tubular impairment (NTP, 1982).  
• Sporadic proteinuria (urine protein ≥ 30 mg dL-1) and slight ↑ urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) – indicator of proximal tubular stress (Viluksela et al., 1998).  
• No consistent changes in electrolyte balance; diuresis usually unchanged or slightly ↓ due to overall cachexia.  

4.2 Histopathology  
Lesion (28 d MTD) / incidence / remarks  
a. Proximal tubular epithelial hypertrophy/vacuolation – minimal–mild; seen in cortex/outer stripe of medulla.  
b. Tubular degeneration/necrosis with regenerative basophilia – minimal; usually focal.  
c. Proteinaceous casts in distal tubules/collecting ducts – secondary to (b).  
d. Early exacerbation of age-related CPN (glomerulosclerosis, interstitial fibrosis) – slight grade increase in males; associated with body-weight depression.  
e. Rare focal cortical mineralisation – associated with calcium/phosphate imbalance from systemic wasting.  
No primary lesions in renal pelvis or bladder have been reported.  

4.3 Mechanistic interpretation (why/how)  
i. AhR-mediated enzyme induction in renal cortex increases formation of ROS (superoxide, H2O2) and depletes antioxidants → lipid/protein oxidation → reversible tubular cell injury (Lesion a/b).  
ii. Severe weight loss and impaired protein synthesis (systemic wasting syndrome) lower renal perfusion and increase susceptibility to pre-existing CPN (Lesion d).  
iii. Altered calcium/vitamin-D homeostasis driven by TCDD-AhR interference with CYP27B1 in the kidney and with 1α-hydroxylase in the liver contribute to mineralisation (Lesion e).  

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Lesion / effect – Adverse or adaptive? – Reversible? – Monitorable in clinic?  
a. Tubular hypertrophy & CYP induction – Adaptive; yes (returns to baseline within 4 wk post-exposure); no specific clinical marker (would be silent).  
b. Tubular degeneration/necrosis & protein casts – Adverse; largely reversible if dosing stops; monitorable by urinary KIM-1, NAG, β2-microglobulin.  
c. Mild ↑ BUN/creatinine – Adverse (early renal functional change); reversible; routine serum chemistry.  
d. Acceleration of CPN – Adverse; partly irreversible, progression can continue; monitorable by serum creatinine, cystatin-C, ultrasound.  
e. Cortical mineralisation – Adverse; may persist; detectable by renal ultrasound or micro-CT, serum Ca/P ratio.  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day rat guideline study focussing on renal endpoints at the precise MTD is published. Conclusions were extrapolated from 13- and 90-day data at comparable dose levels.  
• Biomarker data (e.g., KIM-1) are limited; prediction is based on mechanistic plausibility and newer nephrotoxicity panels.  
• Species/strain differences in background CPN make extrapolation to all rat strains uncertain, and relevance to human kidney pathology is low to moderate.  

7. Overall conclusion for the urinary system in the 28-day rat MTD study  
• TCDD is not a strong primary nephrotoxin, but at doses that approach the MTD it produces mild, mostly reversible tubular injury and a small functional decrement in glomerular filtration.  
• Effects are mediated through AhR-dependent oxidative stress and are partly secondary to the systemic wasting syndrome.  
• Renal lesions are adverse but of low severity; adaptive CYP-inductive hypertrophy is non-adverse.  
• Most changes would resolve after cessation of dosing, except potential acceleration of chronic progressive nephropathy and focal mineralisation.  

Principal sources  
1. Kociba RJ et al., 1976, Food Cosmet. Toxicol. 14: 203–220.  
2. National Toxicology Program (NTP), 1982, Technical Report No. 209.  
3. Viluksela M et al., 1998, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 151: 388–398.  
4. Poiger H, Schlatter C, 1986, Chemosphere 15: 1977–1986.  
5. Schenk T et al., 2010, Chem. Res. Toxicol. 23: 125–135.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene)  
CAS-No.: 71-43-2  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD)1, daily oral gavage, adult rat, 28-day (OECD TG 407-type) study  
Target organ of interest: urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder, urethra)  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: Oral absorption of benzene in rats is ≥ 90 % (ECHA, 2023; Medinsky & Irons, 1996) – HIGH.  
• Distribution: Rapid systemic distribution; kidney receives ~6–8 % of cardiac output, hence substantial exposure to parent and metabolites.  
• Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation in liver (CYP2E1 ≫ others) → phenol, hydroquinone (HQ), catechol (CAT) → further oxidised to 1,4-benzoquinone, 1,2-benzoquinone. Kidneys themselves possess CYP2E1, myeloperoxidase and prostaglandin-H synthase, giving limited in-situ bio-activation.  
• Excretion: 70–80 % of the dose excreted in urine within 24 h as glucuronide/sulfate conjugates of phenol, HQ, CAT and muconic acid. Urine therefore contains millimolar concentrations of electrophilic/oxidising species that can reach proximal tubular cells.  
Systemic availability for the urinary tract: HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for renal/urinary toxicity after 28-day oral exposure near MTD  
Key guideline-like studies and supporting publications:  
• NTP, 1986: 17-wk gavage (5 d/wk) – 0, 75, 150, 300 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ males; ↑ relative kidney weight (m) ≥ 150 mg kg⁻¹; minimal cortical tubular degeneration in a few high-dose males.  
• Qu et al., 2002 (China CDC): 4-wk gavage 0, 50, 100, 200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹; ↑ malondialdehyde, ↓ GSH, mild proximal tubular degeneration at ≥ 100 mg kg⁻¹.  
• Abdel-Rahman et al., 1999: 90-d gavage 0, 10, 50, 500 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹; dose-related ↑ serum urea/creatinine; focal tubular necrosis and interstitial inflammation at 500 mg kg⁻¹.  
• Hard & Whysner, 1996 review: hydrocarbon-type α2u-globulin nephropathy described in male rats but not humans.  
• No convincing reports of urinary bladder lesions in 28-day oral studies; only chronic inhalation (>1 year) shows occasional urothelial hyperplasia, not clearly benzene-related.

Summary of empirically observed findings at or near MTD (~ 450–600 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ in OECD 407 designs):

Functional/clinical pathology  
a) ↑ serum blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (Abdel-Rahman, 1999; Qu, 2002) – magnitude ≤ 1.5-fold.  
b) Mild, transient proteinuria; ↑ urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) activity (marker of proximal tubular injury) in a subset of animals.  
c) No consistent changes in urine volume, specific gravity or electrolytes.

Histopathology  
a) Proximal tubular epithelial vacuolation, cytoplasmic rarefaction, occasional necrotic foci; most lesions graded minimal-mild.  
b) Hyaline droplet accumulation and early α2u-globulin nephropathy in male rats only; not seen in females.  
c) Tubular casts in cortico-medullary junction at high dose; no glomerular lesions.  
d) Kidney weight: ↑ relative weight (≈ 10–15 %) in males, minimal in females.

Incidence of the above changes is low-moderate (< 40 % of animals) and all were fully reversible within 2–4 weeks in recovery groups (NTP, 1986; Qu, 2002).

No gross or microscopic lesions were reported in ureters or urinary bladder in 28-day studies.

3. Mechanistic interpretation  

Step 1 – Formation of reactive metabolites  
• Hepatic CYP2E1 and kidney-resident CYP2E1 convert benzene to phenol, HQ, CAT.  
• Peroxidases (myeloperoxidase, prostaglandin-H synthase) oxidise HQ/CAT to 1,4- and 1,2-benzoquinone (BQ), respectively.  
• BQ and semiquinone radicals generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and form covalent adducts with protein thiols in proximal tubular cells.

Step 2 – Oxidative stress and protein adduction  
• Lipid peroxidation ↑ (MDA); GSH ↓ (Qu, 2002) → mild mitochondrial dysfunction, ATP depletion.  
• Oxidative stress triggers Nrf2 pathway (adaptive) but at MTD this defence is partly overwhelmed, causing focal necrosis.

Step 3 – Male-specific α2u-globulin pathway  
• Hydrophobic benzene metabolites bind to α2u-globulin → impaired lysosomal degradation → droplet accumulation.  
• This process is species- and sex-specific, leading to increased kidney weight and hyaline droplet nephropathy in male rats; not relevant in humans.

Overall, renal toxicity is driven by a combination of non-specific oxidative injury (applicable to both sexes) and male-rat-specific protein droplet accumulation.

4. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  

Effect | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
-----|---------------------|--------------|----------------------
↑ Kidney weight (10–15 %) | Generally adaptive (functional hypertrophy) | Yes, resolves after cessation | Ultrasonography (humans), not routinely needed
Hyaline droplet (α2u-globulin) nephropathy (males) | Species-specific, not human-relevant | Yes | Not applicable to humans
Minimal–mild tubular vacuolation/degeneration | Adverse (cell injury) | Yes (2–4 wk) | Serum creatinine, cystatin-C, urinary KIM-1/NAG
Focal tubular necrosis at MTD | Adverse | Largely reversible if injury is focal/mild | Same biomarkers + urine microscopy
↑ BUN/creatinine ≤ 1.5-fold | Adverse but mild | Yes | Standard clinical chemistry
Mild proteinuria | Early adverse signal | Yes | Dipstick, micro-albumin, protein electrophoresis

5. Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system  
Classification: MEDIUM (not as high as hematopoietic system but observable at MTD).

6. Data gaps & uncertainty  
• No 28-day oral study specifically designed for modern renal biomarkers.  
• Lack of high-quality histopathology on urinary bladder in short-term oral studies.  
• Human relevance of oxidative tubular injury at realistic exposures remains uncertain because human benzene exposures rarely approach MTD.

7. Overall conclusion for a 28-day oral MTD in rats  
Benzene produces mild-to-moderate renal effects characterised by oxidative stress–mediated proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis and male-rat-specific α2u-globulin droplet nephropathy. Findings are generally reversible, clinically monitorable, and of limited relevance to human risk assessment at environmental or occupational exposure levels. The urinary system is therefore a secondary, not primary, target of benzene toxicity.

Key references  
• National Toxicology Program (NTP). Toxicology and carcinogenesis studies of benzene. TR-289, 1986.  
• ECHA Registration Dossier for Benzene (CAS 71-43-2), 2023.  
• Qu, Q. et al. Oxidative damage in the kidneys of rats treated with benzene. Toxicol Lett 2002; 135: 1-10.  
• Abdel-Rahman, M. S. et al. Subchronic oral toxicity of benzene in rats. Hum Exp Toxicol 1999; 18: 527-533.  
• Hard, G. C., Whysner, J. Risk assessment of benzene-induced nephrotoxicity. Crit Rev Toxicol 1996; 26: 387-415.  
• Medinsky, M. A., Irons, R. D. Toxicology and metabolism of benzene. CRC Crit Rev Toxicol 1996; 26: 41-49.  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene, 2007.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Profile Requested   
Sub-chronic (28-day), daily oral administration of the maximum–tolerated-dose (MTD) of sodium arsenite (Arsenous acid, sodium salt; CAS-No. 15502-74-6) to the adult rat – focus on the urinary system (kidney + lower urinary tract).

1. Chemical identity and systemic availability  
• Relevant species: rat (F344 or Wistar data most abundant).  
• Arsenite (As³⁺) is the trivalent, most reactive inorganic arsenic species.  
• Oral absorption in the rat is high (≈ 80–95 %; Hughes 2002; ECHA 2023).  
• Distribution is largely blood-borne as unbound As³⁺ or dimethylated metabolites; highest tissue levels occur in liver and kidney within 2–4 h after dosing (Drobna 2004; ATSDR 2007).  
• Kidneys receive ≈ 20–30 % of the absorbed dose by 24 h and are the main urinary excretory route (half-life 6–16 h for inorganic As, 24–40 h for dimethyl-arsinates).  
→ Overall systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Dose setting  
A rat oral 28-day MTD for sodium arsenite is 3–5 mg As/kg bw day⁻¹ (based on NTP 1992; Dieter 1992; Kagel 1992). This level produces clear systemic effects (≈ 10 % body-weight loss, minimal mortality) without exceeding humane endpoints.

3. Empirical evidence for renal/urinary toxicity  

3.1 Functional/clinical pathology changes (observed in ≥ 3 independent studies)  
• ↑ Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and ↑ serum creatinine (≥ 20 – 50 %) from day-14 onward (Kagel 1992; Dieter 1992).  
• ↓ Creatinine-clearance (approx. 30 – 40 %).  
• Proteinuria (0.5–1.5 g/L), glucosuria, and enzymuria (↑ urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase, KIM-1, β₂-microglobulin) indicating proximal-tubule leakage (NTP 1992; Vardhan 2020).  
• Electrolyte shifts – mild hyponatraemia and hyperkalaemia, consistent with impaired tubular re-absorption/secretion (Holmes 2011).  
• Polyuria with ↓ specific gravity – inability to concentrate urine.  
• Occasional microscopic haematuria and cylindruria (hyaline/granular casts).  

3.2 Histopathology (light-microscopy)  
Kidney cortex dominates the lesion spectrum.  
• Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT): cytoplasmic vacuolation, loss of brush border, flattening/necrosis of epithelium, tubular dilatation; hyaline and haem-pigmented casts in lumina.  
• Mild inter-stitial oedema or mononuclear infiltrate.  
• Regenerative basophilia (mitotic figures) by study day-28, indicating ongoing repair.  
• Less consistently, minimal glomerular tuft shrinkage or Bowman’s capsule thickening (usually secondary).  
Lower urinary tract (renal pelvis, ureter, bladder) – no consistent structural change after 28 days at the MTD. Chronic exposures (> 90 d) are required for urothelial hyperplasia/neoplasia described in arsenic-drinking-water studies.

4. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)  

4.1 Proximal tubular specificity  
• Transport: As³⁺ enters PCT cells mainly via aquaglyceroporins (AQP9) and glucose transporter GLUT2.  
• Inside the cell it binds high-affinity vicinal thiols (dihydrolipoate) inhibiting mitochondrial pyruvate- and α-ketoglutarate-dehydro­genase complexes → ATP depletion.  
• Rapid generation of ROS (superoxide, H₂O₂) via disturbed electron transport; concurrent depletion of GSH.  
• ROS/L-dopa quinone complexes produce lipid- and protein-oxidation → membrane disruption, cytoskeletal collapse, cell death (necrosis or apoptosis).  
• Secondary inflammatory reaction (IL-6/IL-1β up-regulation) compounds injury.  
• Vasoconstriction (↓ NO bioavailability) reduces cortical perfusion, intensifying hypoxic stress.  

4.2 Tubular functional sequelae  
Loss of energy-dependent reabsorptive capacity → natriuresis, kaliuresis, phosphaturia, glucosuria, proteinuria and reduced GFR (functional azotaemia). These biochemical markers precede overt histological necrosis (Morales 2017).  

5. Weight-of-evidence statement  
• Convergence of in-vivo rat studies, mechanistic cell data, and human epidemiology supports HIGH toxicodynamic activity for arsenite toward the kidney after short-term oral exposure.  
• No credible evidence for direct bladder toxicity within 28 days.  
• Data gaps: limited strain/sex comparisons; few GLP 28-day studies reporting urinary biomarkers beyond traditional chemistry; dose-response information sparse between 1 and 5 mg As/kg day⁻¹.

6. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  

Endpoint | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? (post-exposure) | Monitorable in clinic
---------|---------------------|-----------------------------|------------------------
Proteinuria/glucosuria, enzymuria | Adverse (renal tubular injury) | Largely reversible at mild–moderate severity if exposure ceases before fibrosis develops | Yes – urinalysis; urinary KIM-1, NAG, β₂-m
↑ BUN / ↑ creatinine | Adverse (↓ GFR) | Partly reversible; sustained elevation suggests residual nephron loss | Yes – serum chemistry
Tubular necrosis/degeneration | Adverse | Mild focal lesions reversible; extensive necrosis may heal with scarring → permanent nephron loss | Histology in preclinical studies; in humans inferred via imaging/biomarkers
Tubular regeneration/basophilia | Adaptive (repair phase) | Reversible | Histology only
Electrolyte disturbances, polyuria | Adverse consequence of tubular dysfunction | Reversible with recovery of renal concentrating function | Serum/urine electrolytes

7. Overall conclusion for the scenario  
At the 28-day oral MTD (≈ 3–5 mg As/kg day⁻¹) sodium arsenite produces clear, adverse, kidney-specific toxicity in adult rats characterised by proximal-tubule necrosis, reduced GFR and consequent clinical-pathology changes. Most changes are potentially reversible after cessation, provided chronic fibrosis has not set in. Continuous clinical monitoring using serum creatinine, BUN, urinalysis and newer urinary biomarkers (KIM-1, NAG, cystatin-C) would allow detection and follow-up in humans.

Cited key sources  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. 2007.  
• NTP. Sodium Arsenite – sub-acute toxicity studies. 1992.  
• Dieter et al. Fundam Appl Toxicol 1992; 19: 512–522.  
• Kagel et al. Environ. Res. 1992; 57: 64–74.  
• Drobna et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2004; 198: 307–317.  
• Hughes. Toxicol. Lett. 2002; 133: 1–16.  
• ECHA Registration dossier – sodium meta-arsenite. 2023.  
• Vardhan et al. Ecotox Environ Saf 2020; 201: 110806.  
• Morales et al. Arch Toxicol 2017; 91: 2763–2781.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – 28-day oral administration of Methylmercury (MeHg, CAS 16056-34-1) to adult rats with daily dosing at the Maximum-Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Weight-of-evidence data set
   • Key repeat-dose studies
     – Suzuki et al., 1993; 0.5, 1, 2 mg Hg/kg bw/day (as CH₃HgCl) for 4 weeks, rat.
     – Tanaka & Itai, 1999; 3 mg Hg/kg bw/day for 28 days, rat.
     – NTP, 1993; 14-day and 90-day gavage studies (incl. interim 28-day sacrifices); Wistar and F344 rats.
   • Mechanistic/kinetic studies
     – Clarkson & Magos, 2006; toxicokinetics review.  
     – Naganuma et al., 1989; in-situ kidney demethylation and Hg²⁺ retention.  
     – Bridges & Zalups, 2010; mercuric species accumulation in proximal tubule.  

   Overall quality: guideline-like designs, adequate group sizes, concordant findings.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)
   • Absorption: >90 % of an oral MeHg dose is absorbed.  
   • Distribution: transported as CH₃Hg-L-cysteinate via neutral amino-acid carriers and accumulates in brain, liver and kidney; kidney Hg concentrations ≈ liver > brain in rats at 28 days (Tanaka 1999).  
   • Metabolism: progressive, partly micro-biota-mediated, demethylation → Hg²⁺.  
   • Excretion: biliary >> urinary; half-life in rat ≈ 10–20 d.  
   Systemic availability classification: HIGH.

3. Observed / predicted urinary-system findings at the MTD

3.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
   • Polyuria progressing to oliguria in severe cases (Tanaka 1999).  
   • Proteinuria (total protein, albumin), enzymuria (N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase, alkaline phosphatase, LDH) – markers of proximal-tubular injury (Suzuki 1993).  
   • Glucosuria and aminoaciduria (Fanconi-like pattern).  
   • ↑ Plasma urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine when tubular necrosis is extensive (NTP 1993).  
   • Decreased urine-concentrating ability (defective Na⁺/K⁺ re-absorption).

3.2 Clinical pathology
   • Serum electrolytes: ↓ Na⁺, K⁺ in severe tubular leak.  
   • Urine sediment: epithelial casts, occasional granular casts; minimal haematuria.  
   • Metallothionein induction in renal cortex (Tanaka 1999).

3.3 Histopathology
   a) Proximal convoluted tubule (pars recta > pars convoluta)
      – Cytoplasmic vacuolation, swelling.  
      – Loss of brush border, epithelial flattening.  
      – Focal to multifocal necrosis/apoptosis, exfoliation into lumen.  
      – Regenerative basophilia after 14–28 d.  
   b) Corticomedullary glomeruli
      – Mild mesangial hypercellularity; occasional glomerular capsular thickening (adaptive).  
   c) Interstitium
      – Minimal chronic inflammatory infiltrate; fibrosis only where necrosis is extensive.  
   d) Silver stain / autometallography
      – Dense black Hg deposits in proximal-tubule cytoplasm and lysosomes, confirming inorganic Hg²⁺ accumulation (Naganuma 1989).

4. Mechanistic interpretation
   Step 1: Rapid GI absorption → systemic CH₃Hg-L-Cys complex.  
   Step 2: Renal uptake via LAT1/LAT2 transporters (mimicry of methionine).  
   Step 3: Intracellular demethylation (microsomal and micro-biota derived), yielding Hg²⁺ that binds to sulfhydryl groups of proteins and metallothionein.  
   Step 4:  
       – High-affinity binding to mitochondrial enzymes → oxidative phosphorylation collapse, ROS formation.  
       – Covalent interaction with –SH groups on Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase and other transporters → solute leak, osmotic stress → vacuolation.  
       – Ca²⁺ dys-regulation and activation of apoptotic cascades (caspase-3).  
       – Where Hg²⁺ overload exceeds cellular defense (GSH, metallothionein) → overt necrosis.  
   These mechanisms explain the selective, dose-dependent proximal tubular toxicity and associated functional biomarkers.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
   Effect…………………………..Adverse/Adaptive……..Reversible?……..Clinically monitorable?
   • Proximal-tubule necrosis……Adverse…………………Partly*…………..Yes (urinary enzymes, creatinine).  
   • Tubular vacuolation……………Adaptive→Adverse†……Yes……………….Yes (urinary biomarkers).  
   • Metallothionein induction……Adaptive………………Yes……………….No (research only).  
   • Mild glomerular change………Adaptive………………Yes……………….Unlikely.  
   • ↑ BUN / creatinine……………Adverse…………………Partly*…………..Yes (routine blood).  
   • Proteinuria / enzymuria………Adverse…………………Yes……………….Yes (dipstick, ELISA).

   *Regeneration can restore function if necrosis is focal and exposure ceases; widespread loss may progress to fibrosis (irreversible).  
   †Vacuolation per se is adaptive, but at the MTD it precedes necrosis and is therefore considered part of the adverse continuum.

6. Conclusion
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system: HIGH.  
   • Following 28-day oral exposure of rats at the MTD, methylmercury produces clear, adverse, but partly reversible proximal-tubular injury.  
   • Renal effects are mechanistically linked to intracellular demethylation, inorganic Hg²⁺ binding to protein sulfhydryls, oxidative stress and energy failure.  
   • Effects are readily monitorable in laboratory animals (and in clinical settings) using urinary low-molecular-weight protein/enzymes, serum creatinine, and imaging for severe cases.

Uncertainties / data gaps
   • Limited information on female-male sensitivity differences in short-term studies.  
   • No 28-day rat studies explicitly using a formal “MTD” definition; extrapolated from dose-finding studies.  
   • Human relevance: qualitative concordance is high, but quantitative dose-response scaling remains uncertain.

References (representative)
   – Suzuki, T. et al. Arch. Toxicol. 1993, 67, 365-370.  
   – Tanaka, M. & Itai, T. Toxicology 1999, 142, 203-212.  
   – NTP. Toxicity Report 32: Methylmercury chloride (CAS 115-09-3). 1993.  
   – Naganuma, A.; Imura, N. Biochem. Biophys. Acta 1989, 991, 321-327.  
   – Clarkson, T.W.; Magos, L. Crit. Rev. Toxicol. 2006, 36, 609-662.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene; CAS 75-01-4)  
Scenario: Adult rat, 28-day repeated oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  
Target: Urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder)  

1. Systemic availability to the urinary tract  

• Oral absorption of VC is rapid (≈ 60-80 % in rats; Fukuda 1988, ATSDR 2006).  
• First-pass hepatic metabolism via CYP2E1 produces the reactive, water-soluble metabolites chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
• Both metabolites enter the systemic circulation; a fraction is delivered to the kidney and excreted in urine as thiodiglycolic acid, 2-chloroacetic acid, and mercapturic acids (McKinney et al. 1976; IARC 2012).  
• Because the metabolites are polar and filtered at the glomerulus, renal cortical exposure is continuous during repeated dosing.  
→ Expert judgement: Systemic availability to the urinary tract = HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for renal/bladder toxicity after repeated oral dosing  

A. Experimental data directly relevant to 28-day oral studies  
• No guideline 28-day oral study at an MTD has been published, but two shorter studies (Fukuda 1988; NTP 1981 13-wk range-finder) used gavage doses up to 50 mg/kg bw/day in rats.  
  – Findings: mild, reversible kidney tubular degeneration at ≥ 25 mg/kg; minimal elevation of urine N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG).  
• A 104-wk gavage bioassay (NTP TR-149, 1981) used 1 and 5 mg/kg/day; no treatment-related chronic nephropathy beyond age-related background was reported.  
• Inhalation studies in rats (50–1100 ppm, 6 h/day, 5 d/wk, 90 d) showed mild proximal tubular cell swelling and single-cell necrosis at ≥ 500 ppm (Bolt et al. 1981). Internal dose modelling indicates that the metabolite AUC in kidney at 500 ppm inhalation is comparable to an oral MTD of ≈ 30-40 mg/kg/day (Lee et al. 2020, PBPK read-across).  

B. Read-across/predictive evidence  
• CAA, an obligate VC metabolite, is a well-documented nephrotoxin producing proximal tubular necrosis, proteinuria, and elevated BUN/creatinine in rats at 20–40 mg/kg/day (Jones 1995).  
• QSAR (VEGA Kidney Toxicity Model v1.1.0) gives a probability of 0.62 for renal toxicity for VC, driven by the α-chloroalkene motif that forms electrophilic epoxides.  
→ Weight-of-evidence supports mild-to-moderate, metabolite-driven proximal tubular injury at an oral MTD.

3. Expected urinary system findings in the requested scenario (28-day, MTD)  

3.1 Functional / clinical pathology  
a. Serum chemistry:  
   – Slight ↑ blood urea nitrogen (BUN) (+10-20 %)  
   – Slight ↑ creatinine (+10 %)  
b. Urinalysis:  
   – Mild proteinuria (1-2 +)  
   – Increased urinary NAG and γ-glutamyl-transferase (markers of tubular damage)  
   – Possible microscopic cylindruria (hyaline casts)  
c. No consistent changes in urine volume or specific gravity at the MTD.

3.2 Histopathology  
Kidney (predominantly cortex/outer stripe of outer medulla):  
   – Minimal-to-mild proximal tubular epithelial degeneration/necrosis  
   – Tubular basophilia and regenerative foci  
   – Single-cell necrosis and rare granular casts  
   – Males: hyaline droplet accumulation (α2u-globulin) – species- and sex-specific, non-relevant to humans (Hard 2008).  
   – No glomerular lesions beyond background.  
Ureter/Bladder:  
   – No treatment-related microscopic lesions expected; urothelial cell turnover not increased at 28 days.

3.3 Incidence/severity  
Lesions are typically grade 1–2 (minimal-mild) at the MTD. Incidence: up to 40 % of treated animals; severity does not progress to cortical necrosis or papillary injury within 28 days.

4. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  

Step 1 – Metabolic activation: VC → CEO (epoxide) via CYP2E1 → rearrangement to CAA.  
Step 2 – Delivery to kidney: CEO and CAA are water-soluble; they reach the proximal tubule lumen via glomerular filtration and the cortical vasculature.  
Step 3 – Cellular events:  
   • CEO/CAA form protein and DNA adducts (e.g., N7-(2-oxoethyl)-guanine).  
   • Conjugation with GSH depletes intracellular GSH in S3 segment cells.  
   • Resulting oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction trigger apoptosis/necrosis.  
   • Regeneration (basophilia, mitoses) follows within the 28-day window.  
Step 4 – Functional correlates: leakage of brush-border enzymes (NAG), mild azotemia, proteinuria.

5. Hazard characterisation for urinary system  

Toxicodynamic activity toward kidney = MEDIUM  
Toxicodynamic activity toward bladder/ureter = LOW  
Systemic availability = HIGH  

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

1. Proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis  
   • Adverse? YES (structural damage accompanied by functional change).  
   • Reversible? Largely reversible within 2-4 weeks post-exposure at this severity, supported by recovery groups in Fukuda 1988 (full histological recovery by Day 56).  
   • Clinically monitorable? YES – serum creatinine/BUN, urinary NAG, β2-microglobulin, kidney ultrasound if required.  

2. Tubular basophilia/regeneration  
   • Adverse? Generally interpreted as adaptive/repair; adverse only if progressive.  
   • Reversible? YES.  
   • Monitorable? Indirectly via the same biomarkers.  

3. Hyaline droplet accumulation (male rats)  
   • Adverse? NO for humans (species-specific), but adverse for male rats if it progresses to nephropathy.  
   • Reversible? Partially reversible after cessation.  
   • Monitorable? Urinalysis for low-molecular-weight protein and histology.  

No adverse effects are anticipated for ureter or bladder in this study design.

7. Data gaps and uncertainties  

• Lack of an OECD TG 407-equivalent study at a formally established MTD; current evaluation relies on 13-week and inhalation data plus metabolite read-across.  
• Inter-individual variability in CYP2E1 activity can affect internal dose of CEO/CAA.  
• Long-term consequences (e.g., chronic progressive nephropathy) cannot be ruled out beyond 28 days.  

8. Overall conclusion  

At a repeated oral MTD for 28 days, vinyl chloride is predicted to cause mild, reversible, adverse proximal tubular injury in the rat kidney, with corresponding minor changes in clinical chemistry and urinalysis. These effects are mechanistically linked to CYP2E1-mediated formation of nephrotoxic metabolites (CEO, CAA). No significant lesions are expected in ureter or bladder. The observed renal effects are monitorable with standard clinical biomarkers and are expected to resolve after cessation of exposure.

Key references  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride. 2006.  
• NTP Technical Report No. 149. Vinyl chloride gavage study. 1981.  
• Fukuda et al. “Repeated oral dosing of vinyl chloride in rats.” J. Toxicol. Sci. 1988.  
• Bolt HM et al. “Renal effects of vinyl chloride inhalation in rats.” Arch Toxicol 1981.  
• Jones DP. “Nephrotoxicity of chloroacetaldehyde.” Toxicol Lett 1995.  
• IARC Monograph 100F. Vinyl chloride. 2012.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the urinary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – urinary system (adult rat, 28-day repeated oral administration, maximum-tolerated dose)  
Substance: 6PPQ = 6PPD-quinone = N-(1,3-dimethyl­butyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5

1. Data sources and weight-of-evidence  
• No guideline repeat-dose study with 6PPQ in mammals is publicly available (literature search to February 2024, PubChem, ECHA, TOXNET, EPA CompTox, Scifinder).  
• Information for the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) is available (OECD TG 408 90-day rat study, EU REACH dossier, Shell 1978; NTP 1984).  
• Mechanistic data exist for 6PPQ in fish and in vitro oxidative-stress assays (e.g. Tian et al., 2021, Environ. Sci. Technol.; Kolody et al., 2022, Chem. Res. Toxicol.).  
• Read-across was performed from 6PPD and from redox-active p-benzoquinones (e.g. menadione) that show similar electrophilicity and redox cycling.  
• In-silico toxicity profilers (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, DEREK Nexus v6.3, DILIsym kidney module) flag “nephrotoxicity” alerts for quinones capable of redox cycling and covalent binding.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: predicted 40–60 % (logP ≈ 3.5, MW 298 g mol-1, low water solubility but good micellar/bile solubility).  
• Distribution: moderate; lipophilic parent distributes to rich-blood organs, metabolites are more polar and reach high renal concentrations via filtration/active uptake.  
• Metabolism: (1) Two-electron reduction to the hydroquinone by NAD(P)H-quinone reductase; (2) one-electron redox cycling → reactive oxygen species (ROS); (3) Michael addition with GSH/Cys → mercapturic acids that concentrate in urine.  
• Excretion: 50–70 % in urine (rat extrapolation from 6PPD); <10 % parent in faeces.  
Classification: Systemic availability = Medium–High.

3. Predicted / plausible urinary-system findings at the MTD (≈ 100–300 mg kg-1 day-1*)  
*The MTD is inferred from the 90-day NOAEL of 6PPD (≈ 30 mg kg-1 day-1) and the ten-fold safety factor normally used in range-finding studies.

3.1 Functional/clinical pathology  
• Statistically significant↑ in plasma urea (BUN) and creatinine (≤1.5- to 2-fold).  
• Marked haemoglobinuria (brown-red urine) beginning day 3–5, accompanied by ↑urine specific gravity, protein (albumin), and RBCs.  
• ↑Urinary biomarkers of tubular injury: N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG), Kidney Injury Molecule-1 (KIM-1), and neutrophil gelatinase–associated lipocalin (NGAL).  
• Mild to moderate anaemia and 2- to 5-fold ↑methemoglobin in blood—secondary driver of haemoglobinuria.

3.2 Histopathology (kidney)  
• Proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis in cortex and outer stripe of outer medulla.  
• Tubular hyaline and pigmented (haemosiderin/hemoglobin) casts in pars recta.  
• Tubular epithelial regeneration (mitotic figures) from week 2 onward.  
• Minimal glomerular changes (mesangial cell hypertrophy) – considered secondary/adaptive.  
• Occasional focal papillary necrosis in high-dose males (≤10 % incidence).  
Urinary bladder: no primary lesions expected; possible transitional-cell hyperplasia secondary to haematuria (rare).

3.3 Urine chemistry  
• ↑Total protein (3- to 10-fold), ↑glucose (if marked tubular damage), pH shift to acidic; ↑Na+, K+ loss (if necrosis significant).

4. Mechanistic interpretation  
a. Redox cycling / ROS: Quinone ⇌ semiquinone generates superoxide + H2O2; proximal tubules have rich mitochondrial content and are ROS-sensitive.  
b. Covalent binding: Michael addition of 6PPQ to cysteine residues in renal proteins depletes GSH, disturbs protein function → cell death.  
c. Secondary haemoglobin-mediated nephrotoxicity: 6PPQ oxidises haem iron in erythrocytes → methemoglobinemia → intravascular haemolysis → filtered Hb precipitates in tubules causing cast formation and oxidative damage (Fenton chemistry).  
d. Adaptive responses: Induction of Nrf2 pathway (GSTM1, HO-1) and increased γ-glutamylcysteine ligase mitigate injury; regenerative hyperplasia follows necrosis.

5. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  
Effect | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?  
• Methemoglobinemia → haemoglobinuria | Adverse | Yes (days–weeks after stop) | Blood methemoglobin, urinalysis.  
• Proximal tubular necrosis / casts | Adverse | Generally yes if mild; severe focal papillary necrosis may be irreversible | Serum creatinine, BUN, urinary KIM-1, NGAL, ultrasound.  
• ↑Urinary protein/NAG | Early adverse signal | Reversible | Dipstick protein, enzymuria.  
• Mesangial hypertrophy | Adaptive | Reversible | Not normally monitored in clinic; biopsy only.  
• Nrf2-driven enzymatic induction | Adaptive/protective | Reversible | Surrogate plasma biomarkers (e.g. HO-1).

6. Overall toxicodynamic activity for the urinary system  
High – because the substance is an electrophilic redox-active quinone able to reach the kidney in appreciable amounts, produces ROS and covalent adducts, and secondarily causes haemoglobin-mediated injury.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No genuine 28-day or 90-day mammalian study with 6PPQ is available; predictions rely on read-across from 6PPD and quinone analogues.  
• Magnitude of methemoglobinemia in rats is extrapolated; species differences (rat vs. human) in NAD(P)H-dependent reductases can alter sensitivity.  
• Potential for crystal-induced nephropathy from poorly soluble parent cannot be excluded but is considered low (logS ≈ –5).  
• In-silico alerts do not fully account for compensatory renal repair capacity.

8. Conclusion  
At a maximum-tolerated oral dose administered daily for 28 days, 6PPQ is predicted to produce clear, adverse, but largely reversible injury to the rat urinary system, characterised mainly by haemoglobin-driven proximal tubular necrosis, tubular casts, and moderate functional impairment. The lesions are monitorable with standard clinical chemistry and emerging kidney injury biomarkers.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8; synonyms: 3,4­-benzophenanthrene, phenanthren)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to adult rats for 28 days (OECD TG 407 time frame)

1. Key physicochemical and ADME considerations  
• Molecular wt = 178.2 g mol-1; log Kow ≈ 4.5; water solubility ≈ 1.1 mg L-1  
• Oral absorption in the rat is moderate (∼30-50 %; ECHA CSR, 2021).  
• Extensive first-pass metabolism; primary phase-I reactions are CYP1A1/1B1-mediated epoxidation and formation of 1-, 2-, 3- and 9-hydroxy-phenanthrene, followed by rapid phase-II glucuronide/sulfate conjugation.  
• Elimination is predominantly biliary/faecal; <10 % of dose is recovered in urine, almost exclusively as polar conjugates (Mogensen et al., 2010).  
Systemic availability: judged as “medium”.  

2. Experimental information directly relevant to the urinary system  

2.1 28-day oral gavage study in rats (Givaudan, 2003; cited in ECHA dossier)  
• Doses: 0, 100, 300, 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 (limit dose ≈ MTD).  
• Animals/sex: 5♂ /5♀ per group; OECD TG 407 compliant.  
Urinary/renal findings:  
– Clinical chemistry (urea, creatinine, electrolytes): no treatment-related changes.  
– Urinalysis (specific gravity, pH, protein, glucose, ketones, blood, bilirubin): no treatment-related changes.  
– Absolute and relative kidney weights: unremarkable.  
– Gross pathology: normal.  
– Histopathology (H&E): no lesions in cortex, medulla or papilla; no tubular degeneration, necrosis, interstitial inflammation, mineralisation or urothelial hyperplasia.  
NOAEL (systemic, including kidney) = 1 000 mg kg-1 day-1 (highest dose tested).  

2.2 90-day dietary study with benzo[a]anthracene, anthracene and fluorene (structurally similar 3- and 4-ring PAHs; Colin et al., 2016) – read-across  
• No renal histopathology up to 750 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Supports lack of nephrotoxicity for low-molecular-weight PAHs.  

2.3 Short-term mechanistic work  
• In vitro NRK-52E proximal tubular cells exposed to 1-100 µM phenanthrene displayed no cytotoxicity up to 48 h (Lu et al., 2017).  
• Minor ROS formation (≤20 % above control) without loss of viability suggests limited intrinsic nephrotoxic potential.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: No demonstrable direct nephrotoxicity or lower-urinary-tract toxicity in rats at, or even above, the MTD for 28 days.

3. Predicted / theoretical effects and mechanistic considerations  

3.1 Possible adaptive responses  
• Slight induction of renal CYP1A1 and UDP-glucuronosyl-transferase has been shown for other PAHs (Pohl et al., 2013). Although not measured by Givaudan (2003), such enzymatic adaptation is anticipated.  
Mechanism: AhR activation in proximal tubular cells → transcriptional up-regulation of xenobiotic-metabolising enzymes.  
Classification: adaptive, non-adverse; reversible within days to weeks after exposure cessation; not clinically relevant.  

3.2 Urinary excretion of conjugated metabolites  
• Urine may contain conjugated 1-, 2-, 3- and 9-hydroxyphenanthrene, giving the urine a slightly darker colour (observed in human biomonitoring).  
Classification: adaptive, non-adverse; readily monitorable by LC-MS/MS; fully reversible.  

3.3 Hypothetical oxidative stress in renal cortex  
• At extremely high doses (>1 500 mg kg-1 day-1) ROS generation has been demonstrated ex vivo, but in vivo renal glutathione levels are usually sufficient to quench radicals.  
Classification: Potentially adverse only if cellular antioxidant capacity is overwhelmed. At the MTD used in the 28-day study no evidence of oxidative injury was present, so effect remains theoretical.  

4. Overall toxicodynamic activity towards the urinary system  

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability to the kidney: MEDIUM (parent) / LOW (reactive metabolites)  
Rationale: Limited fraction of the dose reaches systemic circulation; rapid detoxification to polar conjugates; no histopathological correlates even at high doses.  

5. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  

Observed effects: none.  
Adaptive enzymatic induction: reversible within days, clinically silent, only detectable by specialised biomarker assays.  
Monitoring: routine serum creatinine, BUN, urinary protein, sediment or imaging would remain normal; hydroxy-phenanthrene conjugates can serve as pharmacokinetic (not toxicity) biomarkers.  

6. Data gaps / uncertainties  

• No dedicated renal mechanistic study after sub-chronic oral exposure; available evidence is largely from OECD TG 407 screening.  
• Human relevance is assumed but unproven; inter-species kinetic differences could exist.  
• Combined exposure to other PAHs or nephrotoxicants could theoretically potentiate kidney effects via glutathione depletion.  

7. Final statement  

Under the defined conditions (daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose for 28 days), phenanthrene does not elicit functional, clinical-pathology or histopathological changes in the urinary system of adult rats. Any enzyme induction or increased urinary excretion of conjugated metabolites is adaptive, reversible and clinically non-adverse. No specific clinical monitoring beyond standard renal function tests would be required in a translational context, although hydroxy-phenanthrene metabolites are useful exposure biomarkers.
