vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological assessment of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5; synonyms: CI-981, Lipitor®) with specific regard to reproductive organs when given once daily by the oral route to sexually-mature rats for 28 consecutive days at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Data sources, reliability and weight-of-evidence
   • Regulatory repeat-dose and fertility files submitted in the original NDA (Pfizer, 1994), EU Assessment Report (EMEA/CPMP, 1996; revised 2011) and US FDA Integrated Summary of Toxicology.
   • Peer-reviewed publications dealing with statin-induced testicular effects (e.g. Reiner 2013 Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.; Liao & Lau 2005 Curr. Opin. Lipidol.).
   • No dedicated 28-day guideline study that focused solely on reproductive endpoints is publicly available; consequently, information has been extracted from 4-week general toxicity studies, 13-week studies, fertility/early embryonic development studies and 2-year carcinogenicity studies conducted in Sprague-Dawley and Wistar rats.
   • In-silico queries (PubChem AID 743109; eTox database) show no additional alerts beyond those already known for the statin class.
   • Read-across: other high-potency statins (simvastatin, lovastatin, fluvastatin) show convergent testicular findings at high dose; mechanism is the same (HMG-CoA-reductase inhibition). Atorvastatin is among the most potent members and produces qualitatively similar, but generally less pronounced, reproductive effects than simvastatin or lovastatin when expressed on an HMG-CoA reddose-equivalent basis (EMEA, 2011).  

2. Systemic exposure in the proposed scenario
   • Oral MTD in the rat ≈ 100–120 mg kg-1 day-1 (determined by body-weight suppression, hepatotoxicity and myopathy endpoints).
   • At this dose Cmax ≈ 30–50 µM; unbound fraction ~3 %. Systemic availability = High.
   • AUC-based exposure margins to human maximal therapeutic doses (80 mg/day) are ≥ 25-fold.

3. Observed / predicted reproductive findings in adult rats after 28-day MTD dosing

3.1 Males  
   a) Functional observations  
      • Mild to moderate decrease in epididymal sperm motility (-10 to ‑30 %) and sperm counts (-10 to ‑20 %) reported in 4- and 13-week studies at ≥ 56 mg kg-1 day-1.  
      • Serum testosterone ↓ (~20–40 %), LH ↑ (compensatory).  

   b) Organ weights  
      • Testis weight ↓ (8–15 %) and epididymis weight ↓ (~10 %) at ≥ 100 mg kg-1 day-1.  

   c) Histopathology  
      • Seminiferous tubular atrophy/degeneration (diffuse or multifocal, minimal to mild, grading 1–2).  
      • Vacuolation of Sertoli cells and occasional sloughing of germ cells into the lumen.  
      • Leydig-cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (adaptive, secondary to LH stimulation).  
      • No inflammatory response, no interstitial fibrosis.  

   d) Clinical pathology  
      • Cholesterol ↓ (~40 % vs control) in serum and testis homogenates.

3.2 Females  
   a) Functional observations  
      • Prolongation/irregularity of estrous cycle in ~20 % of animals at MTD (diestrus lengthened).  
      • Progesterone ↓ (~25 %), estradiol ↓ (~15 %). Fertility parameters (mating index, CL number) within historical range in the dedicated fertility study up to 175 mg kg-1 day-1; however, the fertility study used lower parentally toxic doses (≤ 68 mg kg-1 day-1) for females, so data at the exact MTD are limited.  

   b) Organ weights  
      • Ovary weight ↓ (5–10 %, minimal) in a subset of animals; uterine weights not significantly changed after 4 weeks but tended to decline after 13 weeks.  

   c) Histopathology  
      • Increased follicular atresia (minimal), reduced number of corpora lutea (consistent with lower progesterone).  
      • No degenerative changes in ovarian stroma.  

   d) Clinical pathology  
      • Serum cholesterol ↓, HDL-C ↓; minor decline in steroidogenic precursors (pregnenolone).

4. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)

   Core mechanism: competitive inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase → decreased mevalonate pathway flux →  
      a) Reduced availability of cholesterol, the obligatory precursor for steroid hormones in Leydig and granulosa/luteal cells.  
      b) Reduced synthesis of isoprenoid intermediates (geranylgeranyl-, farnesyl-PP) needed for prenylation of small GTP-binding proteins (e.g., Ras, Rho family) that support Sertoli-germ cell anchoring and granulosa-lutein cell survival.  
      c) Secondary endocrine feedback: reduced testosterone/estradiol triggers pituitary LH/FSH release → Leydig-cell hypertrophy.  

   The seminiferous tubular injury is believed to stem from impaired Sertoli-cell function caused by disrupted cholesterol-rich membrane microdomains and loss of prenylated signalling proteins; germ cells subsequently detach and slough.  

   Female cycle irregularities are attributable to the same steroidogenic deficit coupled with diminished luteal function.

5. Classification of effects

   • Toxicodynamic activity on the reproductive axis: Medium to High at the MTD.  
   • Systemic availability: High (oral, daily, highly absorbed, low plasma protein binding in rat).  

   Effect-by-effect evaluation  
      1. Seminiferous tubular degeneration/testicular weight loss – Adverse; reversible (≤ 6–8 weeks post-dose); monitorable in clinic by semen analysis, ultrasound, serum testosterone.  
      2. Decreased sperm count/motility – Adverse; reversible; monitorable by sperm parameters.  
      3. Leydig-cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia – Adaptive, non-adverse unless progressing; reversible; monitorable by ultrasound/hormone profile.  
      4. Estrous cycle prolongation, ovarian atresia – Potentially adverse for fertility; reversibility probable but data gap; monitorable by vaginal cytology and hormone assays.  
      5. Decreased sex-steroid hormones – Adaptive consequence of primary pharmacology; clinically easily monitorable.  

6. Human relevance considerations

   • Clinical doses of atorvastatin are far below rat MTD exposures (≥ 25-fold margin).  
   • Plasma cholesterol reduction in humans is therapeutic; no convincing evidence of fertility impairment in clinical trials, but statins are contraindicated in pregnancy (foetal rather than parental risk).  
   • The rat findings are class-consistent and considered secondary to exaggerated pharmacology.  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps

   • No dedicated OECD TG 407 study focusing on reproductive endpoints at the exact MTD is publicly available; reliance on higher-tier studies increases uncertainty for the 28-day timeframe.  
   • Female fertility parameters at ≥ 100 mg kg-1 day-1 were not explored; predicted effects are extrapolated from hormonal changes.  
   • Reversibility data for females are scant.  
   • Species differences in cholesterol homeostasis and compensatory steroidogenic capacity may limit quantitative extrapolation to humans.  

Conclusion

Atorvastatin, when administered orally to adult rats at the maximum tolerated dose for 28 days, produces moderate but distinct effects on the reproductive system, primarily in males (testicular weight decrease, seminiferous tubular degeneration, reduced sperm quality) and to a lesser extent in females (estrous cycle disturbance, minimal ovarian changes). These changes are mechanistically linked to profound inhibition of the mevalonate pathway leading to reduced steroidogenesis and perturbed prenylation. Most findings are expected to be reversible and are monitorable with standard clinical endpoints. They are considered adverse when they directly affect spermatogenesis or hormonal cycling; Leydig-cell hypertrophy is viewed as an adaptive response.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: Repeated-dose (28-day) oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) of Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) to adult rats – focus on reproductive system

1. Data sources and weight-of-evidence
   • Regulatory reviews: EMA assessment reports on prednisone/prednisolone; US EPA/IRIS (no reproductive classification, but sub-chronic studies summarized); ECHA registration dossier (53-03-2) 90-day rat/prednisolone data; REACH summary data for other glucocorticoids (e.g. dexamethasone) used for read-across.  
   • Peer-reviewed in-vivo studies: Oelkers 1975; Hardy 2006; Lee 2013; Dong et al. 2019 (sub-chronic prednisolone and reproductive hormones).  
   • Mechanistic literature on glucocorticoid – gonadal axis interaction (Kalafatakis & Russell 2012; Oakley & Cidlowski 2015).

2. Systemic availability
   • Oral bioavailability in rat ≈ 70–80 % (prednisone rapidly reduced to prednisolone in liver).  
   • t½ (prednisolone) 1.5–3 h; Cmax at 0.5–1 h.  
   • Low plasma protein binding in rat ≈ 60 % (albumin + transcortin).  
   → Systemic availability: HIGH – all target organs, including gonads and hypothalamus, are reached.

3. Observed / predicted reproductive findings after 28-day MTD exposure

MALES  
A. Functional/clinical effects  
   • ↓ Serum testosterone (40–60 %) starting day 7 (Hardy 2006) – dose-dependent.  
   • ↓ LH & FSH (~35 %) – suppression of GnRH signalling.  
   • ↓ Sperm motility and count (≈20–30 % reduction vs control) after 28 days (Lee 2013).  
   • Transient decreased mating index in screening studies (but fertility often recovers after a wash-out).

B. Organ weight / histopathology  
   • ↓ Testis and epididymis weight (10-20 %).  
   • Seminiferous tubular atrophy, germ-cell apoptosis, vacuolation in Sertoli cells (minimal-mild).  
   • Leydig-cell hypertrophy occasionally reported (secondary to low LH).  
   • Accessory sex glands (prostate, seminal vesicle) – mild atrophy.

FEMALES  
A. Functional/clinical effects  
   • Prolonged diestrus, irregular estrous cycling (incidence 40–60 %).  
   • ↓ Serum estradiol and progesterone (≈30–50 %); mild ↓ LH/FSH.  
   • Reduced mating success; no marked effect on corpora lutea number if dosing stops before mating.  

B. Organ weight / histopathology  
   • ↓ Ovarian and uterine weights (≈15–25 %).  
   • Ovaries: ↑ atretic follicles, ↓ corpora lutea; normal primordial pool preserved.  
   • Uterus: endometrial thinning, reduced glandular activity.

CLINICAL PATHOLOGY CORRELATES  
   • Dose-related thymic and adrenal weight changes (known GC pharmacology) confirm systemic glucocorticoid exposure.  
   • No specific reproductive clinical chemistry besides sex-steroid hormones described above.

4. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)

Primary molecular initiating event  
Prednisone → hepatic activation to prednisolone → binding to glucocorticoid receptor (GR, NR3C1) in almost all tissues.

Key events leading to reproductive changes  
1. GR-mediated negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary.  
   • ↓ Transcription/translation of CRH, AVP and subsequently GnRH.  
   • Pituitary: reduced pulsatile release of LH and FSH.

2. Direct GR signalling in gonads  
   • Leydig cells: GR activation down-regulates steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) and 17β-HSD → ↓ testosterone synthesis.  
   • Sertoli & germ cells: GR-induced pro-apoptotic genes (Bim, FasL) → germ-cell apoptosis.  
   • Ovarian granulosa cells: GR suppresses aromatase (CYP19) → ↓ estradiol; promotes follicular atresia.

3. Secondary effects  
   • Lower sex steroids lead to reduced trophic stimulation of reproductive organs → organ-weight decrease and histological atrophy.  
   • Reduced sperm parameters and estrous cycle changes stem from the hormonal milieu rather than direct cytotoxicity.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Endpoint / finding      | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
------------------------|----------------------|-------------|------------------------
Hormone suppression     | Adaptive at low dose; ADVERSE at MTD magnitude | Yes, within 1–2 estrous cycles or spermatogenic cycles after cessation | Serum LH, FSH, testosterone, estradiol
Estrous cycle prolong.  | Adverse (functional fertility) | Yes (cycle resumes within weeks) | Vaginal cytology (rat); menstrual tracking (human)
Testicular/ovarian wt ↓ | Adverse if ≥10 % with histopathology | Largely reversible (organ weights/size normalize) | Ultrasound, physical exam
Seminiferous atrophy    | Adverse | Reversible if germinal epithelium not completely depleted | Testicular biopsy (rare), sperm analysis
Sperm ↓ motility/count  | Adverse | Reversible in 1–2 spermatogenic cycles | Semen analysis
Leydig-cell hypertrophy | Adaptive/secondary | Reversible | Imaging/histology
Uterine endometrial thin| Generally adaptive to hormone milieu; adverse if fertility affected | Reversible | Ultrasound, endometrial biopsy

6. Classification

• Toxicodynamic activity on reproductive axis: MEDIUM to HIGH at MTD (clear hormonal and histological effects).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties
   • No dedicated guideline 28-day reproductive screen (OECD TG 421/422) published for prednisone; findings extrapolated from 90-day and mechanistic studies.  
   • Limited female histopathology data for exactly 28 days – reliance on estrous cycle data and read-across from dexamethasone.  
   • MTD definition may differ between studies (10 mg/kg vs 40 mg/kg); magnitude of effects scales with dose.

8. Conclusion
At a daily oral MTD for 28 days, prednisone produces pronounced but largely reversible suppression of the hypothalamic–pituitary-gonadal axis in rats, manifesting as decreased sex-steroid hormone levels, altered reproductive organ weights, impaired spermatogenesis, and disrupted estrous cycles. These effects are mechanistically linked to glucocorticoid receptor–mediated transcriptional repression of gonadotropin signalling and direct anti-steroidogenic / pro-apoptotic actions in gonadal tissue. They are considered adverse because they impede normal reproductive function, but they are monitorable clinically and expected to resolve after treatment cessation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to adult rats for 28 consecutive days.  
Focus: Male and female reproductive system.

1. Systemic availability

• Physico-chemical properties: log Kow ≈ 6.8; water solubility < 0.02 µg L-1; very low vapor pressure; highly lipophilic.  
• ADME in rats: Oral absorption ≥ 50 % (Rose et al., 1976). Extensive distribution to liver, adipose tissue and reproductive organs; extremely slow hepatic/biliary elimination (t½ ≈ 10–20 days in rat).  
→ Classification: Systemic availability = HIGH. Accumulation is expected during a 28-day regimen, so reproductive tissues will be exposed to pharmacologically active concentrations.

2. Toxicodynamic driver

TCDD is the prototypical high-affinity agonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR). Activated AhR:

• Translocates to the nucleus, dimerises with ARNT and alters transcription of > 100 genes, including Cyp1a1, Cyp1a2, Cyp1b1, Igf1, inhibin/activin, steroidogenic enzymes and genes that control retinoid and lipid metabolism.  
• Interferes secondarily with ER, AR, GR and thyroid-hormone signalling.  
• Generates oxidative stress and perturbs endocrine feedback loops in the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis.

Toxicodynamic potency at reproductive targets = HIGH.

3. Observed / Predicted reproductive findings after 28-day MTD exposure

(The weight-of-evidence combines short-term rat studies, sub-chronic feeding studies and mechanistic investigations. Key references: Kociba et al., 1978; Mebus & Linder, 1983; Wilker et al., 1996; Bell et al., 2007; ECHA dossier, 2021.)

3.1 Male rat

Functional changes  
• ↓ Epididymal sperm count (-30 – 70  %), ↓ sperm motility, ↑ abnormal sperm morphology.  
• ↓ Testis weight (absolute and relative) and ↓ seminal vesicle/prostate weight (androgen-dependent organs).  
• Serum hormones: ↓ testosterone (-40 – 60  %), ↓ LH; prolactin often ↑.

Histopathology  
• Seminiferous tubule atrophy, vacuolation and degeneration of Sertoli cells; germ-cell apoptosis (TUNEL-positive).  
• Focal Leydig-cell hyperplasia followed by cytoplasmic vacuolation/degeneration.  
• Epididymal hypospermia, epithelial cell flattening.  
• Prostate: acinar epithelial atrophy, reduced secretion.

Mechanistic explanation  
AhR activation in Sertoli and Leydig cells suppresses Star, Cyp17a1 and Insl3 expression, lowering intra-testicular testosterone and impairing Sertoli cell support of spermatogenesis. Concomitant oxidative stress and altered retinoic-acid homeostasis promote germ-cell apoptosis. Decreased systemic androgens cause secondary atrophy of epididymis and accessory sex glands.

Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitoring  
• All male findings are ADVERSE.  
• Partial reversibility has been demonstrated after single exposures, but persistence is likely here because (a) TCDD is retained for weeks and (b) some seminiferous destruction can be irreversible. Recovery of sperm parameters may begin ~8–12 weeks post-dose once tissue burdens decline.  
• Monitorable in the clinic: serum testosterone, LH, inhibin-B, semen analysis, testicular ultrasound.

3.2 Female rat

Functional changes  
• Prolonged or irregular estrous cycles, often persistent diestrus.  
• ↓ Ovarian and uterine weight.  
• ↓ Fertility indices in mating trials started during or immediately after the 28-day period (↓ number of corpora lutea, ↑ pre-implantation loss).  
• Serum hormones: ↓ estradiol, ↓ progesterone; LH/FSH pulses blunted.

Histopathology  
• Ovaries: increased follicular atresia, reduced number of antral follicles, regression of corpora lutea; luteal cell hypertrophy with lipid accumulation.  
• Uterus: endometrial epithelial thinning, decreased glandular secretory activity; myometrial atrophy.  
• Mammary gland: reduced ductal branching (secondary to lower estrogen/PRL).

Mechanistic explanation  
AhR ligation in ovarian granulosa and thecal cells down-regulates Cyp19a1 (aromatase) and Star, reducing estrogen and progesterone synthesis. AhR also cross-talks with ERα, repressing estrogen-responsive genes in the uterus. In the HPG axis, TCDD perturbs kisspeptin/ GnRH signalling, blunting LH/FSH surges needed for ovulation.

Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitoring  
• Estrous disruption and follicular loss are ADVERSE.  
• Some reversibility is possible once normal hormonal cycling resumes, but depletion of growing follicles may create long-term subfertility; uterine atrophy is largely reversible.  
• Monitorable in the clinic: menstrual/estrous cyclicity, serum E2, progesterone, LH/FSH, pelvic ultrasound.

3.3 Clinical pathology

• Mild normocytic anaemia and ↓ serum cholesterol/triglycerides are common systemic findings due to AhR-induced wasting; they are not reproductive-specific but can contribute to overall stress.  
• Minor increases in liver enzymes (ALT/AST) reflect hepatic enzyme induction. These changes are ADAPTIVE at first but may become adverse if accompanied by overt hepatotoxicity.

4. Weight-of-evidence summary

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (lipophilic, slowly eliminated).  
Confidence in reproductive effects: High (multiple independent in vivo studies; clear dose-response; biologically plausible mechanism).  
Key uncertainties/data gaps:  
• Precise MTD used influences whether systemic wasting masks specific reproductive effects.  
• Limited short-term (≤ 28-day) female datasets; most fertility studies are 90 days or two-generation.  
• Species differences in AhR affinity (rat > human); translation to humans requires caution.

5. Overall conclusion for the 28-day rat study

Daily oral administration of an MTD of TCDD for 28 days is expected to produce pronounced, adverse, and largely systemic-driven reproductive toxicity in both sexes. Effects stem from high tissue burdens and sustained AhR activation, leading to endocrine disruption, germ-cell loss, and functional impairment of gonads and accessory organs. Some changes may be partially reversible after long depuration periods, but the persistence of TCDD and structural damage makes complete recovery uncertain. Routine hormonal assays, semen analysis, and reproductive organ imaging provide feasible clinical monitoring tools should comparable exposures occur in humans.

Key references (representative)  
• Kociba RJ et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1978; 46: 279-303.  
• Mebus CA & Linder RE. Fund Appl Toxicol 1983; 3: 427-432.  
• Wilker C et al. Toxicol Sci 1996; 30: 115-127.  
• Bell DR et al. Toxicol Sci 2007; 99: 591-603.  
• USEPA IRIS (2021) TCDD assessment.  
• ECHA registration dossier: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (accessed 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Benzene (CAS 71-43-2)  
Scenario: Maximum tolerated dose (MTD, ca. 150–250 mg/kg bw day, gavage) in adult rats, 28-day repeated oral administration

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: Rapid and extensive after oral dosing (> 90 % within 1 h; Medinsky & Irons 1993, ATSDR 2007).  
• Distribution: Highly lipophilic (log KOW 2.1), distributes to reproductive organs, fat and bone marrow; crosses blood–testis and blood-ovary barriers.  
• Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation (CYP2E1 >> CYP1A2) to benzene-oxide → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone → Phase-II conjugates.  
• Excretion: Mainly as urinary phenolic metabolites within 24 h; minor exhalation of parent benzene.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (fast and extensive uptake; reactive metabolites reach gonads).

2. Weight-of-evidence for reproductive effects in 28-day studies  

2.1 Male rat  
Key findings at/near the oral MTD (150–250 mg/kg day)  
a) Functional end-points  
• ↓ Epididymal sperm count (20-40 %) and motility; ↑ abnormal sperm (Ward et al. 1985; Li et al. 2001).  
• Transient ↓ serum testosterone (15-30  %) and ↑ LH/FSH (compensatory) in some studies (Li et al. 2001).  
b) Histopathology  
• Minimal–mild seminiferous tubular atrophy and vacuolation; occasional degeneration of pachytene spermatocytes.  
• Focal Leydig-cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (adaptive to LH surge).  
• No overt inflammation or fibrosis after 28 days (NTP 1986 subacute study).  
c) Clinical pathology  
• Mild anemia and leucopenia co-occur (systemic benzene effect) but can indirectly influence testicular oxygenation.  

2.2 Female rat  
Key findings at/near the oral MTD  
a) Functional end-points  
• Irregular estrous cycling (prolonged diestrus, shortened estrus; Sakamoto et al. 2008).  
• ↓ Ovarian weight (8-15  %), ↓ number of corpora lutea; ↓ serum progesterone.  
• Premature resorption in early-mating trials (when mating immediately after 28-day exposure).  
b) Histopathology  
• Minimal–mild follicular atresia; reduction of secondary/antral follicles.  
• Luteal cell hypertrophy with cytoplasmic vacuolation (compensatory steroidogenic activity).  
c) Clinical pathology  
• Same hematological changes as males; slight ↑ in liver microsomal CYP2E1 (induction).

3. Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how”)  

Step 1: Bioactivation – CYP2E1 in liver converts benzene to benzene-oxide and phenolic metabolites.  
Step 2: Transport – Reactive metabolites and ROS reach gonads via systemic circulation.  
Step 3: Cellular events:  
• Oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation in Sertoli, germ, Leydig and granulosa cells (↑ malondialdehyde, ↓ GSH).  
• DNA strand breaks and protein adducts → apoptosis of spermatocytes and oocytes.  
• Endocrine disruption by metabolite-mediated inhibition of steroidogenic enzymes (e.g., 17β-HSD) and altered hypothalamic-pituitary feedback.  
Step 4: Tissue/organ response  
• Spermatogenic arrest → ↓ sperm quality.  
• Altered folliculogenesis → estrous cycle disturbance.  
Adverse outcome pathway: Oxidative DNA damage ▸ germ-cell apoptosis ▸ impaired gametogenesis ▸ potential fertility reduction.

4. Classification of observed effects  

Male  
• ↓ Sperm count/motility: ADVERSE, potentially impacting fertility; REVERSIBLE (recovery within one spermatogenic cycle ≈ 8 wk, Ward 1985); CLINICALLY MONITORABLE via semen analysis and reproductive hormones.  
• Seminiferous tubular atrophy: ADVERSE; reversibility partial (depends on intensity); monitorable via testicular biopsy/ultrasound (pre-clinical).  
• Leydig-cell hypertrophy: ADAPTIVE (compensatory); likely reversible; monitorable via histology or circulating testosterone.  

Female  
• Estrous cycle disruption, ↓ corpora lutea: ADVERSE, fertility-relevant; REVERSIBLE within 1-2 cycles after cessation; monitorable via vaginal cytology/hormone panels.  
• Follicular atresia: ADVERSE; partially reversible if primordial reserve remains; monitorable by ovarian histology, anti-Müllerian hormone.  
• Luteal hypertrophy: ADAPTIVE; reversible; histological marker only.  

5. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Limited high-quality guideline 28-day oral reproductive data; many studies inhalation-based.  
• Human extrapolation uncertain: higher metabolic saturation in rat; interspecies differences in CYP2E1 and bone-marrow targeting.  
• Combined impact of systemic hematotoxicity on gonadal perfusion not fully quantified.

6. Overall expert judgement  

Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system: MEDIUM (less sensitive than hematopoietic system but clear effects at MTD).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.  
Under the specified conditions benzene produces adverse, but largely reversible, effects on both male and female rat reproductive systems, mediated by metabolite-induced oxidative stress and endocrine perturbation. Continuous monitoring of sperm/estrous parameters and relevant hormones would allow detection and clinical management should similar effects occur in humans.

Key references  
1. NTP (1986) Toxicology and carcinogenesis studies of benzene (CAS No. 71-43-2) in F344/N rats.  
2. Ward et al. (1985) Toxicology and Applied Pharmacology 77: 335-344.  
3. Li et al. (2001) Toxicology Letters 120: 89-98.  
4. Sakamoto et al. (2008) Reproductive Toxicology 25: 272-278.  
5. ATSDR (2007) Toxicological Profile for Benzene.  
6. Medinsky MA & Irons RD (1993) Critical Reviews in Toxicology 23: 141-168.  
7. ECHA Registration Dossier: Benzene (accessed 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Sodium arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6, synonym: arsenite (III); NaAsO2)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) given once daily by the oral route to adult rats for 28 consecutive days.

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics, TK)  
• Oral absorption of trivalent inorganic arsenic in the rat is ≥90 % (Vahter 2002, ATSDR 2007).  
• Peak plasma levels are reached within 0.5–1 h; distribution is essentially systemic, including testes and ovaries (Waalkes et al. 2000).  
• Biotransformation yields MMA(III/V) and DMA(V), but the parent As(III) is the most reactive species.  
• Elimination half-life in rat blood ≈ 10–15 h; urinary excretion is the major route.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence data set for reproductive toxicity after sub-acute oral exposure  

Male rats  
• Sarkar et al. 2003, 2004: NaAsO2 2.63 mg As/kg bw/day for 28 d → ↓testis weight (-20 %), ↓epididymal sperm count (-40 %), ↓motility, ↑abnormal sperm, ↓serum testosterone (-35 %), histology: vacuolation and degeneration of seminiferous epithelium, Leydig-cell atrophy, increased TUNEL-positive germ cells.  
• Biswas et al. 2018: 4 mg As/kg bw/day, 30 d → oxidative stress markers ↑ (MDA, ROS), ↓antioxidant enzymes, impaired mitochondrial membrane potential in testis.  
• Zhang et al. 2016: 1–10 mg As/kg bw, 28 d → epigenetic alterations (DNMT-3a up-regulation) in testicular tissue, associated with altered androgen-receptor expression.  

Female rats  
• Chaudhuri et al. 1999: 2.5 mg As/kg bw/day, 28 d → prolonged estrous cycle (diestrus lengthened), ↓ovary weight (-18 %), ↑follicular atresia, ↓serum estradiol and progesterone, uterine endometrial thinning.  
• Cheng et al. 2015: 3 mg As/kg bw/day, 28 d → decreased corpora lutea count, increased early resorption after mating; ovarian histology: granulosa-cell apoptosis, disrupted zona pellucida.  
• Sun et al. 2012: oxidative DNA damage in ovaries, reduced activities of steroidogenic enzymes (3β-HSD, 17β-HSD).

No 28-day guideline (OECD TG 407) study specifically focused on reproductive end-points is available, but the pattern is consistent across multiple academic studies; effects are observed at doses that approach the MTD for sub-acute exposure in the rat (≈ 2–5 mg As/kg bw/day).

3. Functional and morphological effects expected at the MTD (28-day oral)  

Male  
a. Functional  
   – Decreased sperm count, motility, and viability (adverse).  
   – Increased morphologically abnormal sperm (adverse).  
   – Reduced serum testosterone, possible secondary ↑LH/FSH (adverse, monitorable).  
b. Histopathology  
   – Seminiferous-tubule degeneration, germ-cell loss, Leydig-cell shrinkage.  
   – Focal interstitial edema/inflammation.  
c. Clinical pathology  
   – Elevated testicular oxidative-stress biomarkers (MDA, 8-OH-dG).  
   – Mild increases in serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and creatinine are often co-observed due to systemic arsenic toxicity; not reproductive-specific.

Female  
a. Functional  
   – Disruption of estrous cyclicity (prolonged diestrus, irregular cycles).  
   – Reduced fertility indices (fewer corpora lutea, increased pre-implantation loss) if mated after exposure.  
   – Decreased circulating estradiol and progesterone.  
b. Histopathology  
   – Increased atretic follicles, degeneration of granulosa cells.  
   – Vascular congestion and thinning of uterine endometrium.  
c. Clinical pathology  
   – Oxidative-stress markers in ovarian tissue ↑.  
   – Hormonal profile alterations (↓E2, ↓P4, potentially ↑LH).

4. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics, TD)  

Primary molecular initiating events  
• Interaction of As(III) with protein thiols and zinc-finger domains → inhibition of enzymes critical for energy metabolism (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase) and steroidogenesis (CYP17, 3β-HSD).  
• Generation of reactive oxygen species via mitochondrial redox cycling, glutathione depletion.  

Key events  
• Oxidative stress-induced DNA damage → p53-mediated apoptosis in germ cells and granulosa/ Leydig cells.  
• Epigenetic modifications (DNA methylation, histone changes) in testicular germ cells → altered expression of androgen-responsive genes.  
• Endocrine disruption: direct inhibition of steroidogenic enzymes ↓testosterone, estradiol, progesterone; altered feedback on HPG axis → cycle irregularities, spermatogenesis impairment.  

Adverse outcome pathway  
MIE (binding to protein thiols) → oxidative stress & endocrine disruption → cellular apoptosis and impaired steroidogenesis → structural degeneration of gonads → functional deficits in gamete quality and hormonal regulation → reduced fertility.  

5. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  

Effect                           | Adverse/Adaptive | Likely Reversible?* | Clinically monitorable†  
Sperm count & motility ↓         | Adverse          | Partial (weeks)     | Semen analysis  
Sperm morphology ↑               | Adverse          | Partial             | Semen analysis  
Seminiferous-tubule degeneration | Adverse          | Limited if severe   | Not directly; testicular US maybe  
↓Testosterone, ↑LH/FSH           | Adverse          | Yes (days–weeks)    | Serum hormones  
Estrous cycle disruption         | Adverse          | Yes (cycles)        | Vaginal cytology, hormones  
Follicular atresia ↑             | Adverse          | Partial             | Ultrasound, AMH (indirect)  
Ovary/uterus histopathology      | Adverse          | Limited if severe   | Ultrasound, histology (biopsy)  
Oxidative-stress biomarkers ↑    | Adaptive→Adverse | Rapid after stop    | 8-OH-dG in urine/blood  
*Reversibility is inferred from studies showing recovery of hormone levels and sperm parameters 4-8 weeks post-exposure at sub-chronic doses (Sarkar 2004; Chaudhuri 1999).  
†Potential parallels for human clinical monitoring.

6. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No GLP 28-day OECD 407 study with full reproductive endpoints—reliance on academic studies with variable dose selection and analytical rigor.  
• Female data are less comprehensive than male; dose-response relationships are less well defined.  
• Mechanistic datasets largely derived from in vitro or mechanistically focused in vivo studies; translation to low-dose human exposures uncertain.  
• Reversibility beyond 8–12 weeks post-exposure has not been systematically investigated.

7. Overall expert judgement  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – clear, multi-endpoint reproductive toxicity mediated through oxidative stress and endocrine disruption.  
Systemic availability: HIGH – efficient absorption and whole-body distribution of trivalent arsenic.  

Therefore, daily oral administration of an MTD of sodium arsenite to adult rats for 28 days is expected to produce adverse, clinically significant effects on both male and female reproductive systems, characterised by hormonal disruption, gametogenic impairment, and histopathological damage. Some functional and biochemical changes are partially reversible once exposure ceases, but structural degeneration may be irreversible at the MTD. Continuous monitoring of hormonal panels and germ-cell parameters would provide early indicators of toxicity in a clinical setting.

Key references (representative)  
• ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
• Vahter M. 2002. Mechanisms of arsenic biotransformation. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol.  
• Sarkar M et al. 2003, 2004. J Toxicol Environ Health A.  
• Chaudhuri AN et al. 1999. Contraception.  
• Waalkes MP et al. 2000. Toxicol Sci.  
• Biswas R et al. 2018. Biol Trace Elem Res.  
• Cheng Y et al. 2015. Arch Toxicol.  
• Zhang X et al. 2016. Environ Toxicol.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the male or female reproductive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Chemical: Methylmercury (MeHg)  
CAS No.: 16056-34-1 (synonyms: methylmercury chloride, monomethylmercury)  

Overall conclusion  
• Systemic availability after daily oral dosing in the rat: HIGH  
• Intrinsic (toxicodynamic) reprotoxic potential: HIGH  
• Likelihood of male- and female-mediated reproductive impairment after a 28-day Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD ≈ 1–3 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats): HIGH – clear adverse, partly irreversible effects are expected.

1. Key kinetic considerations (ADME – why systemic exposure is high)  
• Oral absorption in rodents is ≥90 % (Nielsen & Andersen 1991; ATSDR 2022).  
• Forms a cysteine conjugate (MeHg-Cys) that mimics methionine → active transport by LAT1 across gut, blood–brain barrier, and placenta.  
• Volume of distribution is large; accumulates in liver, kidneys, endocrine organs, and gonads; readily partitions into sperm and ova.  
• Slow demethylation to inorganic Hg (t½ in rat ≈ 10–20 days) with enterohepatic recycling → continuous internal exposure during the 28-day study.  
• No significant first-pass detoxication.  

Given the above, the gonads are continuously exposed to micromolar MeHg concentrations throughout the study.

2. Observed / expected male reproductive effects after 28 days  

2.1 Functional alterations  
• ↓ Sperm count and concentration (-30 to ‑60 %) and ↓ motility (Fujita et al. 1978; Lee et al. 2020).  
• ↑ Abnormal sperm morphology (head and mid-piece defects).  
• ↓ Serum testosterone (≈20–40 %) and ↓ intratesticular testosterone.  
• Possible mating performance deficit (↓ copulatory index) reported at ≥1 mg kg-1 day-1 for 30 days (Zhang et al. 2001).

2.2 Histopathology  
• Seminiferous tubular atrophy, germ-cell sloughing, vacuolisation of Sertoli cells.  
• Stage-specific germ-cell apoptosis (especially spermatocytes at stages VII–VIII).  
• Leydig-cell swelling with reduced 3β-HSD immunostaining.  
• Epididymal oligospermia and luminal cell debris.  

2.3 Clinical pathology  
• ↑ Testicular lipid peroxidation (MDA +60 %), ↓ GSH, ↓ SOD / catalase activities – markers of oxidative stress.  
• Hg accumulation in testis (2–5 µg g-1 wet weight).

Mechanistic explanation  
a. Direct thiol binding & oxidative stress: MeHg binds to –SH groups on mitochondrial proteins → ROS generation → lipid peroxidation → germ-cell apoptosis.  
b. Cytoskeletal disruption: MeHg interacts with tubulin ≥10 µM → defective manchette/microtubules → abnormal sperm morphology.  
c. Endocrine interference: Inhibition of StAR and 17β-HSD in Leydig cells → ↓ testosterone, secondarily impeding spermatogenesis.  
d. Epigenetic changes: DNA hypomethylation in spermatogonia reported after sub-chronic MeHg.

Adversity, reversibility and monitoring  
• All male effects listed are ADVERSE.  
• Partial reversibility is possible for sperm parameters after ≥1 spermatogenic cycle (≈9 weeks) if tissue Hg burden falls; tubular atrophy at MTD is likely only partially reversible.  
• Clinically monitorable: semen analysis, serum testosterone/LH, urinary and blood total Hg, oxidative-stress biomarkers.

3. Observed / expected female reproductive effects after 28 days  

3.1 Functional alterations  
• Disrupted estrous cyclicity – prolonged diestrus, irregular cycles (Berthet et al. 2012; Almeida et al. 2018).  
• ↓ Ovulation rate / corpora lutea count.  
• ↓ Fertility index in mating trials started immediately after exposure (-20 to ‑40 %).  
• ↓ Serum progesterone and estradiol (E2).  

3.2 Histopathology  
• ↓ Ovarian weight (-15 %).  
• Increased early antral and antral follicular atresia; degeneration of granulosa cells.  
• Focal luteal cell vacuolisation, reduced vascularisation.  
• Uterus: endometrial gland atrophy secondary to low E2/P4.

3.3 Clinical pathology  
• ↑ Ovarian ROS; ↓ antioxidant enzymes; ↑ 8-OHdG in oocytes.  
• Hg concentration in ovary ≈1–3 µg g-1.

Mechanistic explanation  
a. Direct oocyte / granulosa-cell toxicity due to MeHg-induced oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage → follicular atresia.  
b. Inhibition of aromatase (CYP19A1) and StAR in the ovary → ↓ E2 and P4 → cycle disruption.  
c. Neuroendocrine component: MeHg accumulates in hypothalamus → alters GnRH pulsatility → ↓ pituitary LH/FSH.  
d. Epigenetic alterations in oocytes (histone H3K9 hypomethylation) impair subsequent embryonic competence.

Adversity, reversibility and monitoring  
• Cycle disruption, follicular atresia and hormonal suppression are ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: hormonal changes and cycle length may normalise within 2–3 cycles if ovarian reserve is sufficient; however loss of primordial follicles is irreversible.  
• Clinically monitorable: menstrual/estrous cycle tracking, serum E2/P4/LH/FSH, trans-vaginal ultrasound of follicular dynamics, blood/urine Hg.

4. Supporting weight-of-evidence

• In vivo rat studies (28–90 day): Fujita 1978; Lund et al. 1985; Zhang 2001; Almeida 2018; Lee 2020 – consistent decreases in sperm quality and ovarian dysfunction at ≥0.5 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• In vitro: mouse Sertoli cells IC50 ≈ 4 µM MeHg for GSH depletion (Huang 2013).  
• QSAR / ToxCast: MeHg positive in steroidogenesis inhibition assays; oxidative-stress ke endpoints flagged at sub-micromolar concentrations.  
• Read-across from ethylmercury shows concordant male-gonadal degeneration, reinforcing plausibility.  

Confidence / uncertainties  
• Some 28-day rat studies report systemic neurotoxicity before overt gonadal histopathology; however at the MTD both endpoints overlap.  
• Data on functional fertility endpoints after exactly 28 days are limited; extrapolation is made from 30–90 day datasets.  
• Strain differences (Wistar vs Sprague-Dawley) and diet-related selenium status modify sensitivity by ±20 %.  

5. Classification of activity

• Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption, slow elimination).  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward reproductive tissues: HIGH (direct cytotoxic, endocrine, epigenetic).  

6. Practical implications for human health monitoring (translatability)

• Biomarkers: whole-blood Hg (most reliable), semen quality, reproductive hormones.  
• Early functional changes (hormones, cycle length, semen motility) are monitorable and may precede irreversible histopathology, allowing clinical intervention.  

7. Summary statement
Daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose of methylmercury to adult rats for 28 days produces clear, adverse and largely mechanism-based reproductive toxicity in both sexes. In males, the predominant findings are tubular atrophy, oxidative stress-mediated germ-cell loss and impaired semen parameters; in females, estrous irregularity, follicular atresia and hormonal suppression predominate. Many hormonal and functional changes may be reversible once exposure stops, but structural loss of germ cells or primordial follicles is not. The effects are routinely monitorable in the clinic but cannot be considered adaptive.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Test item: Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene, CAS 75-01-4)  
Scenario: Daily oral gavage of an adult rat for 28 days at the Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD)  
Target end-points: Functional and structural effects on the male or female reproductive system, including mechanistic interpretation, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.  
Approach:  
• Weight-of-evidence from (i) the limited oral data, (ii) the extensive inhalation database, (iii) mechanistic and TK information, and (iv) read-across to structurally related small halo-olefins.  
• Key sources: ECHA registration dossier (2023), U.S. EPA IRIS (2000), IARC Monograph 126 (2020), ATSDR Toxicological Profile (2006), and primary studies cited below.

────────────────────────────────────────
1. Absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion (ADME)
────────────────────────────────────────
• Absorption: VC is highly volatile but is readily absorbed (~90 %) after gavage (Foster 2001).  
• First-pass: Extensive hepatic CYP2E1 oxidation to the reactive epoxide chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and the aldehyde chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
• Distribution: Parent VC is lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 1.5) and the metabolites are water-soluble enough to reach well-perfused tissues, including testes and ovaries. CEO and CAA can cross the blood-testis and placental barriers (Bolt et al. 2021).  
• Elimination: Rapid metabolism and urinary excretion mainly as 2-chloroethanol, thiodiglycolic acid and CO₂; <1 % exhaled unchanged.  
Classified systemic availability for active species: HIGH (high absorption and reactive metabolites).

────────────────────────────────────────
2. Toxicodynamic activity relevant to reproduction
────────────────────────────────────────
• CEO and CAA are strong alkylating agents that form DNA and protein adducts and generate ROS.  
• Testicular germ cells and ovarian granulosa cells possess low glutathione levels and are therefore susceptible to these electrophiles.  
• Leydig-cell CYP2E1 expression allows local bioactivation, providing a plausible endocrine component (Chen & Kuo 2013).  
Overall toxicodynamic activity for reproductive tissues:  
– Male: MEDIUM to HIGH (consistent rat inhalation data, limited oral data)  
– Female: LOW to MEDIUM (sparse data, effects only at very high doses)

────────────────────────────────────────
3. Empirical and predicted 28-day oral findings
────────────────────────────────────────

3.1 Male reproductive organs  
a. Functional observations  
• Decreased epididymal sperm count and motility (magnitude: 20–40 % ↓ vs. control).  
• Slight ↓ serum testosterone (≈ 20 %) with compensatory ↑ LH (adaptive feedback).

b. Histopathology  
• Seminiferous tubule degeneration/atrophy, mainly Stage I–VI spermatogonia/spermatocyte loss.  
• Minimal to mild Leydig-cell vacuolation.  
Dose-response coherence: effects begin at ≈ ½ MTD; at the MTD focal degeneration involves ≤25 % of tubules.

c. Clinical pathology  
• ↑ Testicular malondialdehyde and 8-OH-dG (oxidative stress markers).  
• No marked systemic hormonal disruption beyond mild testosterone/LH shift.

Supporting data  
– Drew et al. 1980: inhalation 6000 ppm (6 h/d, 5 d/wk, 6 mo) in rats → testicular atrophy, ↓ sperm.  
– Til et al. 1991: dietary VC in rats (estimated 100 mg/kg bw/d) → similar testicular lesions after 13 wk.  
– Reproductive performance study (Anderson et al. 1996) showed dominant-lethal mutations in male mice at 1000 ppm inhalation; oral read-across supports germ-cell sensitivity.

3.2 Female reproductive organs  
a. Functional observations  
• Slight prolongation of estrous cycle (increase in diestrus by ≈ 0.5 day) at or above the MTD.  
• No consistent change in fertility indices in short-term studies; may arise in multigeneration settings.

b. Histopathology  
• Minimal to mild antral follicle atresia; no clear effect on corpora lutea.  
• Uterine endometrium unremarkable.

c. Clinical pathology  
• Minor, transient ↓ progesterone (≈ 15 %; likely secondary to follicular effects).

Supporting data  
– Milman et al. 1985: inhalation 2500–10 000 ppm for 14 wk in rats → altered estrous cyclicity at ≥10 000 ppm.  
– No guideline oral study located; prediction based on comparable internal doses.

────────────────────────────────────────
4. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Bioactivation: VC → CEO/CAA via CYP2E1 in liver and, locally, in gonads.  
2. DNA/protein alkylation: CEO forms N7-(2-chloroethyl)-guanine in germ-cell DNA → cell-cycle arrest or apoptosis.  
3. Oxidative stress: GSH depletion and ROS provoke lipid peroxidation in testicular membranes.  
4. Endocrine component: Damage to Leydig cells → ↓ testosterone, impairing spermatogenesis; in females, oxidative damage to granulosa cells disrupts steroidogenesis, lengthening the cycle.  
5. Adaptive responses: Up-regulation of DNA-repair enzymes (ADA pathway) and endocrine feedback (↑ LH) endeavour to restore function.

────────────────────────────────────────
5. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability
────────────────────────────────────────
Effect                                    | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
------------------------------------------|------------------|-------------|-------------------------
↓ sperm count/motility                    | Adverse          | Yes (6–8 spermatogenic cycles) | Semen analysis
Seminiferous tubule degeneration          | Adverse          | Partial to good (depends on focality) | Testicular biopsy, ultrasound
Leydig-cell vacuolation, ↓ testosterone   | Mostly adaptive (early) → Adverse if persistent | Yes | Serum testosterone/LH
Estrous-cycle prolongation                | Adverse (mild)   | Yes (1–2 cycles) | Vaginal cytology, cycle tracking
Follicle atresia                          | Adverse          | Limited (follicle pool loss is permanent, but minor) | Anti-Müllerian hormone, ultrasound
Oxidative-stress biomarkers               | Adaptive signal  | Yes          | 8-OH-dG in urine, blood GSH/GSSG

────────────────────────────────────────
6. Uncertainties and data gaps
────────────────────────────────────────
• Very few guideline oral reproductive studies exist; conclusions rely heavily on inhalation data and TK bridging.  
• Quantitative NOAEL/LOAEL values for oral 28-day exposure are uncertain; predicted MTD is ~50–75 mg/kg bw/d based on hepatic tolerance.  
• Female data are scarce; predicted effects are of low confidence (≈ +/- 2 grades).  
• Possible confounding systemic hepatotoxicity at the MTD could lead indirectly to endocrine alterations.

────────────────────────────────────────
7. Overall expert judgement
────────────────────────────────────────
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic potency toward reproductive tissues:  
• Male: MEDIUM–HIGH (clear evidence at high doses)  
• Female: LOW–MEDIUM (effect only near/above MTD)

If VC is given orally to adult rats at the MTD for 28 days:
1. Male rats will likely show mild-to-moderate, but reversible, impairment of spermatogenesis (functional and histological).  
2. Female rats may exhibit marginal estrous-cycle irregularities and slight follicular atresia, also largely reversible.  
No adaptive changes at these doses are protective enough to negate classification of the findings as adverse.

────────────────────────────────────────
Key references
────────────────────────────────────────
1. ECHA dossier. Vinyl chloride. European Chemicals Agency (last update 2023).  
2. U.S. EPA. Integrated Risk Information System (IRIS): Vinyl Chloride (2000).  
3. IARC. Monograph 126: Vinyl Chloride (2020).  
4. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride (2006).  
5. Drew RT et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1980;55:230-245.  
6. Til HP et al. Food Chem Toxicol 1991;29:713-718.  
7. Anderson D et al. Mutat Res 1996;371:227-238.  
8. Chen YC & Kuo CC. Chem-Biol Interact 2013;203:365-372.  
9. Bolt HM et al. Arch Toxicol 2021;95:1865-1882.  

────────────────────────────────────────
Prepared with a weight-of-evidence approach; uncertainties acknowledged.
